properties.quick_answer: The lot is the crux of this hike. A small dirt pull-in off Highway 138 just south of Camp Seeley in Crestline — roughly 8–10 cars — with an Adventure Pass required on the dash. On weekends from March through June it's full by 9am. Camp Seeley's road is private, and Highway 138's shoulders are narrow and patrolled.

properties.fill_time: Spring/early-summer weekends: by 9am
properties.overflow: None sanctioned — weekdays are the real answer
properties.tow_risk: Citations on Hwy 138 shoulders; Camp Seeley road is private

sections.heading: The situation
sections.body: Heart Rock's Instagram fame collides with a trailhead built for a quieter era: a small dirt parking area off Highway 138 near the Camp Seeley entrance, room for about 8–10 cars, Adventure Pass required. When the waterfall is flowing — March through June — weekend demand outstrips those spaces by an order of magnitude. What doesn't work: the paved road into Camp Seeley is private camp property (they will ask you to leave), and Highway 138's shoulders near the trailhead are narrow, signed, and patrolled — citations are routine on busy weekends.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: Honestly: go on a weekday or before 9am. That's the whole strategy. If you arrive to a full lot on a weekend, the best move is to flip the day's order — Lake Gregory is 10 minutes away with big paid lots, Crestline has breakfast — then check the trailhead again after 3pm when the morning wave clears. For the trail itself — distance, the falls, the sketchy final scramble — see the full Heart Rock trail guide on our sister site.

faq.q: Where do you park for Heart Rock Trail?
faq.a: In the small dirt lot off Highway 138 just south of the Camp Seeley entrance in Crestline, with an Adventure Pass displayed. It holds roughly 8–10 cars.

faq.q: What if the Heart Rock lot is full?
faq.a: There is no sanctioned overflow. Camp Seeley's road is private and Highway 138 shoulders are enforced. Come back on a weekday, arrive before 9am, or return after 3pm when the morning crowd leaves.

faq.q: Do you really need an Adventure Pass at Heart Rock?
faq.a: Yes — it's a fee site, and rangers do ticket. $5/day at local vendors and ranger stations, $30 for the annual; an America the Beautiful pass also covers it.

properties.name: Deep Creek Hot Springs Trailheads
properties.meta_title: Deep Creek Hot Springs Parking: Bowen Ranch vs Bradford Ridge (2026)
properties.meta_description: Parking for Deep Creek Hot Springs: the Bowen Ranch dirt lot ($10/person cash, rough road from Apple Valley) versus free but limited roadside parking at the Bradford Ridge Path off Hwy 173. Which to pick and what each costs.
properties.card_summary: Two ways in: Bowen Ranch's dirt lot ($10/person cash, rough road) or free, limited roadside at Bradford Ridge off Hwy 173.
properties.quick_answer: Your parking choice picks your route. Bowen Ranch (from Apple Valley): dirt lot on private ranch land, $10 per person, cash at the gate, rough dirt-road drive in, shortest hike down. Bradford Ridge Path (from Hwy 173 north of Lake Arrowhead): free, but roadside parking is limited and unsigned. Neither trailhead has water, facilities, or shade worth mentioning.
properties.trail_served: Deep Creek Hot Springs (~5 mi RT either route, hard climb out)
properties.fee: Bowen Ranch: $10/person cash. Bradford Ridge: free (forest land)
properties.lot_size: Bowen Ranch: dirt ranch lot. Bradford Ridge: a handful of roadside spots
properties.fill_time: Weekend middays are busiest at both; weekdays easy
properties.overflow: None at either — this is remote country, not a lot system
properties.tow_risk: Keep clear of travel lanes on Hwy 173; ranch parking is at the ranch's rules
properties.road_access: Bowen Ranch: miles of washboard dirt from Apple Valley. Bradford Ridge: paved Hwy 173
properties.best_season: October–May; summer heat here is genuinely dangerous

sections.heading: Bowen Ranch side (Apple Valley)
sections.body: The most popular access crosses private Bowen Ranch: drive washboard dirt roads out of Apple Valley, pay $10 per person, cash, at the gate, park in the ranch's dirt lot, then hike ~2.5 miles down to the creek. The fee surprises people every weekend — it's private property, not a Forest Service site, so no pass covers it. Low-clearance cars survive the road but hate it. The ranch also allows paid camping if you're making a night of it.

sections.heading: Bradford Ridge side (Hwy 173)
sections.body: From the mountain side, the Bradford Ridge Path starts off Highway 173 north of Lake Arrowhead and drops ~2.6 miles to the creek. Parking is free — it's national forest — but it's informal roadside space for a handful of cars, unsigned and easy to miss. Park fully clear of the travel lanes. Whichever side you park on, read the safety notes — no shade, 100°F+ summer afternoons, and a keep-your-head-above-water rule at the springs — in the full Deep Creek guide on our sister site.

faq.q: How much is parking for Deep Creek Hot Springs?
faq.a: Via Bowen Ranch: $10 per person (not per car), cash only, paid at the ranch gate. Via the Bradford Ridge Path off Highway 173: free. The springs themselves are national forest with no fee.

faq.q: Which Deep Creek trailhead has better parking?
faq.a: Bowen Ranch has an actual lot but a rough dirt-road drive and the $10/person fee. Bradford Ridge is free with a paved-highway approach, but only a few informal roadside spots. On a busy weekend, Bowen Ranch absorbs more cars.

faq.q: Do I need an Adventure Pass for Deep Creek?
faq.a: No — Bowen Ranch is private property (the $10 is their fee), and the Bradford Ridge roadside isn't a designated fee site. Neither route uses an Adventure Pass lot.

sections.heading: Finding the lot
sections.body: Turn off Highway 18 onto Keller Peak Road immediately after the Deer Lick Fire Station (between Skyforest and Running Springs); the small trailhead lot is a short way down the road. USFS lists the trail as 2W09X. It's primitive — no water, no restrooms, room for a handful of cars — but unlike the famous trailheads in the range, space is rarely the limiting factor. What is: closures. The Keller Peak area has had fire-related closures in 2026. Check the San Bernardino NF alerts page or call the Mountaintop Ranger District before committing to the drive.

sections.heading: The shortcut nobody mentions
sections.body: If you want the Keller Peak fire-lookout view without the ~12-mile round-trip hike, Keller Peak Road is paved all the way to the lookout and open seasonally — you can simply drive up. Trail details and the full hike description are in the Exploration Trail guide on our sister site.

faq.q: Where is the Exploration Trail trailhead?
faq.a: On Keller Peak Road just off Highway 18, immediately past the Deer Lick Fire Station between Skyforest and Running Springs. Small primitive lot; Adventure Pass required.

faq.q: Is the Exploration Trail open?
faq.a: It has been affected by fire-related closures in 2026. Check the San Bernardino National Forest alerts page or call the Mountaintop Ranger District before going.

sections.heading: Why this one's easy
sections.body: Heaps Peak Arboretum sits on the Highway 18 corridor halfway between Lake Arrowhead and Running Springs, and its paved lot is sized for the attraction — an easy ~1-mile interpretive loop through the largest giant sequoia grove in the San Bernardinos. Even on summer weekends it usually has space, which makes it the reliable backup plan when Heart Rock's lot has already burned you. The one rule people miss: walking the trail is free, but parking in the Forest Service lot requires an Adventure Pass (or America the Beautiful pass) on the dash. There's no booth — just signage and rangers who do write tickets.

sections.heading: Worth knowing
sections.body: Vault restrooms at the lot, dawn-to-dusk access every day, and a volunteer information booth on weekends 11am–3pm. The trail is stroller-tolerant and grandparent-approved — full walk details in the Heaps Peak guide on our sister site.

faq.q: Does Heaps Peak Arboretum parking cost anything?
faq.a: The trail is free, but the lot is a Forest Service fee site: Adventure Pass ($5/day, $30/year) or an interagency pass on the dash. No booth — honor system with ranger enforcement.

faq.q: Does the Heaps Peak lot fill up?
faq.a: Rarely — it's the most reliable trailhead parking on the Hwy 18 corridor. Even summer weekends usually have space.

sections.heading: Parking reality
sections.body: The Pinnacles — the granite towers northwest of Lake Arrowhead with roughly 500 bolted climbing routes — never got a developed trailhead. Hikers and climbers park in informal dirt pullouts off Highway 173 north of the lake; the mapped Forest Service OHV staging area in the same area is the closest thing to an actual lot. There's no signage for the Pinnacles at any of it. That's why the standard advice here is digital, not vehicular: have the approach route downloaded offline (AllTrails, Gaia) before you leave pavement, because finding where to park is the first route-finding problem of the day.

sections.heading: One honest caveat
sections.body: Because nothing here is signed or developed, "the right pullout" varies by source and season. Our pin marks the staging-area vicinity — treat it as a starting point, cross-check your climbing or hiking beta, and if you find better intel, the Mountain Project page is where locals keep it current. Full scramble details in the Pinnacles guide on our sister site.

faq.q: Where do you park for the Arrowhead Pinnacles?
faq.a: Informal dirt pullouts off Highway 173 north of Lake Arrowhead, near the Forest Service OHV staging area. There's no developed lot, no signage, and no facilities — download your approach route before you go.

faq.q: Do I need a pass at the Pinnacles?
faq.a: An Adventure Pass covers Forest Service parking areas like the staging area. Wherever you stop, park fully clear of Highway 173's travel lanes.

sections.body: This is the trailhead where parking is not the story: a small area near the North Shore Campground entrance on Hospital Road that has space on all but the busiest days. Display an Adventure Pass and pick your trail — the North Shore Recreation Trail descends into shady forest for a moderate 4-mile round trip, while Little Bear Creek Trail is the area's default bring-the-dog, bring-the-kids walk. Campground restrooms operate in season. Full trail write-ups live on our sister site: North Shore Recreation Trail and Little Bear Creek Trail.

faq.q: Does the North Shore trailhead fill up?

A paved 38-space facility (2 ADA) at Big Bear Boulevard and Talbot Drive, free, no Adventure Pass needed, open 7am–7pm with hours enforced. sections.heading: The new lot fixed the chaos (mostly)
sections.body: For years, Castle Rock parking meant cars jammed along a blind curve of Big Bear Boulevard. That ended in July 2025: the City of Big Bear Lake opened a purpose-built lot at Talbot Drive with 38 paved spaces (2 ADA), a restroom building, trash service, and a bike rack — and with it came the other half of the fix: since July 23, 2025, parking along Big Bear Boulevard near the trailhead is prohibited and strictly enforced, with a $100 fine. Park in the lot or don't park. The lot is free with no Adventure Pass required — a genuine rarity in this range — with posted hours of 7am–7pm.

sections.heading: It still fills
sections.body: Thirty-eight spaces meet Big Bear's most popular trail: on summer and fall weekends the lot is typically full by 9am. With roadside banned, there's no shrug-and-improvise option — the play is to arrive before 8:30am, come on a weekday, or swing back after mid-afternoon when the morning wave clears. For the trail itself — the scramble, the lake views, why everyone's here — see the full Castle Rock Trail guide on our sister site.

faq.q: Where do you park for Castle Rock Trail in Big Bear?
faq.a: In the dedicated lot at 38501 Big Bear Boulevard at Talbot Drive — 38 paved spaces, free, open 7am–7pm. Since July 2025, parking along the road near the trailhead is prohibited.

faq.q: Do you need an Adventure Pass at Castle Rock?
faq.a: No — the Castle Rock lot is free with no pass required, unlike most developed trailheads in the San Bernardinos.

faq.q: What if the Castle Rock lot is full?
faq.a: There's no legal overflow — roadside parking near the trailhead carries a $100 fine. Arrive before 8:30am on weekends, hike on a weekday, or return after mid-afternoon.

sections.heading: The mismatch
sections.body: Cougar Crest is Big Bear's gateway to the Pacific Crest Trail — one trailhead serving the Cougar Crest Trail, Bertha Peak, and Delamar Mountain — parked by a lot that fits 10 to 15 cars. It needs an Adventure Pass on the dash (a pit toilet is the only facility), and on summer and fall weekends it's full by 8:30am. The demand-to-spaces ratio here is among the worst in the range.

sections.heading: The plan B that's actually plan A
sections.body: Six-tenths of a mile east on Highway 38 sits the Big Bear Discovery Center: free parking, 50+ spaces, real restrooms, and staff who sell Adventure Passes if you need one for another day. From its lot, the paved Alpine Pedal Path runs through a tunnel under the highway straight to the Cougar Crest trailhead — a flat 10–12 minute walk that saves $5 and the 8am scramble. One catch: the Center is closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays, and its lot serves center visitors during open hours (roughly 9am–4pm) — so for closed days or a dawn start, it's the trailhead lot, early. Every other day, start at the Center. Trail details for all three summits are in the Cougar Crest guide on our sister site.

faq.q: Where do you park for the Cougar Crest Trail?
faq.a: The trailhead lot on North Shore Drive holds only 10–15 cars and requires an Adventure Pass. On days the Big Bear Discovery Center is open (closed Tue–Wed), most people are better off parking free there, 0.6 miles east, and walking the paved Alpine Pedal Path to the trailhead.

faq.q: When does the Cougar Crest lot fill up?
faq.a: By about 8:30am on summer and fall weekends. The Discovery Center's larger free lot is the reliable alternative.

faq.q: Do you need an Adventure Pass for Cougar Crest?
faq.a: At the trailhead lot itself, yes. Parking at the Big Bear Discovery Center instead is free — and the Center sells Adventure Passes if you need one.

sections.heading: Why start here
sections.body: The Discovery Center is the San Bernardino National Forest's visitor hub on Big Bear's north shore, and its free 50+ space lot quietly solves the area's worst parking problem: instead of fighting for one of Cougar Crest's 10–15 spaces with an Adventure Pass on the dash, park here free and walk the paved Alpine Pedal Path through the tunnel under Highway 38 — about 0.6 flat miles to the trailhead. Inside (during open hours): restrooms, free trail maps, Adventure Pass sales, and staff who know current trail conditions. The catch is the schedule: the Center is closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays (open Mon/Thu/Fri 9am–4pm, weekends 8:30am–4:30pm — confirm at (909) 382-2790). On closed days, or for an alpine start, don't build your plan around this lot — use the trailhead lot early instead.

faq.q: Is parking free at the Big Bear Discovery Center?
faq.a: Yes — 50+ paved spaces, no Adventure Pass required. It's one of the best free parking options in Big Bear.

faq.q: How far is the walk from the Discovery Center to the Cougar Crest trailhead?
faq.a: About 0.6 miles on the paved Alpine Pedal Path, which passes through a tunnel under Highway 38 — a flat 10–12 minute walk.

sections.heading: The civilized trailhead
sections.body: Pine Knot's trailhead doubles as the Aspen Glen Picnic Area, and it shows: a real day-use lot with 20–30 spaces, vault toilet, picnic tables, BBQ grills, and a community fire ring under the Jeffrey pines at about 7,000 feet. An Adventure Pass goes on the dash — this one is a proper fee site. The good news is the demand curve: this lot rarely fills before 9am, even on summer weekends, making it the reliable choice when Castle Rock's lot has already burned you. The payoff at the top — Grand View Point's panorama of the San Bernardino high country — is in the full Pine Knot Trail guide on our sister site.

faq.q: Where do you park for the Pine Knot Trail?

faq.q: Does the Pine Knot lot fill up?

properties.name: Humber Park / Devil's Slide (Idyllwild)
properties.meta_title: Humber Park Parking (Idyllwild): Adventure Pass, the 30-Permit Quota & Tow Reality (2026)
properties.meta_description: Parking for Devil's Slide and Tahquitz Peak at Humber Park in Idyllwild: Adventure Pass required, a separate free wilderness permit with a 30-per-day summer weekend quota, and Fern Valley Road towing that runs $400+. The full stack, explained.
properties.card_summary: The most rule-stacked trailhead we cover: Adventure Pass to park, a free wilderness permit to hike, a 30/day summer weekend quota — and real towing on Fern Valley Rd.
properties.quick_answer: Two different pieces of paper, and the permit is the harder one. Parking at Humber Park (end of Fern Valley Rd, ~6,400 ft) needs an Adventure Pass — but hiking Devil's Slide also needs a separate free San Jacinto Wilderness day-use permit, capped at 30 per day on summer weekend days (Sat–Sun, Memorial Day–Labor Day). Roadside on Fern Valley Rd is signed no-stopping and tows are real. The permit-free fallback from the same lot: the Ernie Maxwell Scenic Trail.

sections.heading: The paperwork stack
sections.body: Humber Park is the most rule-stacked trailhead we cover, and people get burned by treating it as one requirement instead of two. To park: an Adventure Pass (or Interagency pass) on the dash — buy it in town, not at the lot. To hike Devil's Slide: a separate, free San Jacinto Wilderness day-use permit from the ranger station at 54270 Pine Crest Ave in Idyllwild — a self-issue kiosk outside the office covers non-quota days 24/7. The crunch is summer weekends: Saturdays and Sundays from Memorial Day through Labor Day, Devil's Slide is capped at 30 day-use permits per day. Reserve up to 90 days ahead by phone — (909) 382-2921 — or take your chances same-day. Rangers check at the trailhead on quota weekends. On those days the permit, not the parking, is usually what ends people's plans.

sections.heading: Parking reality and the tow trucks
sections.body: The lot itself is paved, in two sections (lower by the Ernie Maxwell trailhead, upper by the restrooms and Devil's Slide), at about 6,400 ft at the end of Fern Valley Road. No official space count is published, so we won't invent one — hiker consensus is simply that summer weekend mornings demand a sunrise arrival. Do not improvise on Fern Valley Road: it's signed no-stopping near the park entrance with seasonal no-parking below, and climbers' forums document tows off the road and even the lower lot — roughly $440 to retrieve your car from town, with citations around $114. There is no sanctioned overflow; downtown Idyllwild is about two miles back down the road.

sections.heading: The permit-free plan B
sections.body: If the quota's gone or the day got away from you, the Ernie Maxwell Scenic Trail leaves from the same lot's lower section: ~2.6 miles one-way of shaded forest toward town, no wilderness permit required per the Forest Service's San Jacinto district trail brochure (one USFS webpage says otherwise — the brochure and every practical source disagree; call (909) 382-2921 if you want it from the horse's mouth). The Adventure Pass still applies to the lot either way. One more 2026 note: Highway 243 into Idyllwild has an active Caltrans paving project with 15–20 minute flag delays into winter — budget for it.

faq.q: Do you need a permit for the Devil's Slide Trail?
faq.a: Yes — a free San Jacinto Wilderness day-use permit from the Idyllwild ranger station (self-issue kiosk on non-quota days). On summer weekend days (Sat–Sun, Memorial Day–Labor Day) it's capped at 30 permits per day; reserve up to 90 days ahead at (909) 382-2921.

faq.q: Where do you park for Tahquitz Peak?
faq.a: Humber Park at the end of Fern Valley Road in Idyllwild — Tahquitz Peak is reached via Devil's Slide to Saddle Junction, so the same Adventure Pass and wilderness-permit rules apply.

faq.q: Do you need an Adventure Pass at Humber Park even without entering the wilderness?
faq.a: Yes. The Adventure Pass covers the parking itself and applies whichever trail you hike. It's separate from the wilderness permit and isn't sold at the lot — buy it in town first.

faq.q: What happens if you park on Fern Valley Road?
faq.a: The stretch near Humber Park is signed no-stopping with seasonal no-parking below, and enforcement is real — documented tows run about $440 plus a citation. If the lot is full, there is no sanctioned overflow.

properties.name: Vivian Creek / Falls Picnic Area (Forest Falls)
properties.meta_title: Vivian Creek Trailhead Parking (Forest Falls): Pass, Online-Only Permit & the Free Overflow (2026)
properties.meta_description: Parking for Vivian Creek and San Gorgonio at the Falls Picnic Area: Adventure Pass required, a free online-only wilderness permit (release 90 days out), a lot shared with Big Falls waterfall tourists — and an official overflow at Momyer that needs no pass at all.
properties.card_summary: San Gorgonio's front door: Adventure Pass + an online-only wilderness permit, a lot shared with waterfall tourists — and a free official overflow at Momyer.
properties.quick_answer: Three things to know before the drive. Parking at the Falls Picnic Area on Valley of the Falls Drive needs an Adventure Pass; hiking Vivian Creek into the San Gorgonio Wilderness needs a separate free permit — online only at permits.sgwa.org (released 90 days out, no in-person issuance); and on busy summer weekends the lot closes when full, with the official overflow at Momyer Trailhead — which, unlike this lot, is free to park.

badges.label: Lot closes when full — no roadside

properties.trail_served: Vivian Creek Trail 1E08 → San Gorgonio Peak; Big Falls Trail 1E13 (0.3 mi waterfall walk) from the same lot
properties.fee: Adventure Pass ($5/day, $30/yr) or Interagency pass — not sold on site; SGWA wilderness permit free but required
properties.lot_size: Trailhead lot + additional picnic-area spaces; no official space count published
properties.fill_time: Summer weekend mornings: hikers report dawn arrivals finding few spots; weekdays far easier
properties.overflow: Official: Momyer Trailhead (free, no pass), ~3 mi up Valley of the Falls Dr from Hwy 38, then an uphill road walk — no roadside parking anywhere on the drive
properties.tow_risk: USFS is explicit: NO parking along Valley of the Falls Drive or the picnic-area entrance; Forest Falls is residential — don't test it
properties.road_access: Paved: Hwy 38 to Valley of the Falls Drive; day use sunup–sundown at the picnic area (6,080 ft)
properties.best_season: May–October for San Gorgonio; waterfall crowds peak spring–early summer
properties.address: Falls Picnic Area, Valley of the Falls Drive, Forest Falls, CA 92339

sections.heading: Two documents, one dashboard, one phone
sections.body: The Falls Picnic Area lot is San Gorgonio's front door: Vivian Creek — the shortest route up the tallest peak in Southern California — starts here, and so does the 0.3-mile Big Falls waterfall walk, which means peak-bagger dawn patrol and waterfall tourists compete for the same spaces. An Adventure Pass (or Interagency pass) is required and not sold on site — buy before you drive up. The wilderness permit is the part that surprises people: every hike into the San Gorgonio Wilderness, day hikes included, needs a free permit, and it's issued online only at permits.sgwa.org — the ranger station does not hand them out in person. Quota permits release 90 days ahead, popular summer weekends genuinely sell out, and rangers check PDFs on the trail. (At least one popular hiking blog claims day hikers don't need one — the Forest Service and SGWA are unambiguous that they do.)

sections.heading: When the lot fills — the official plan B
sections.body: The Forest Service's own page says it plainly: on busy summer weekends the site closes when parking hits capacity, and there is no parking along Valley of the Falls Drive or at the picnic-area entrance — this is a residential canyon, not an overflow zone. Hiker reports from summer weekends describe dawn arrivals finding only a few spots, so a San Gorgonio summit day means an early start on parking grounds alone. The sanctioned fallback is Momyer Creek Trailhead, a large lot about three miles up the drive from the Highway 38 turnoff — and here's the quirk worth knowing: Momyer has no parking fee at all, no Adventure Pass needed. The trade is an uphill road walk back to the picnic area (use extreme caution on the road, per the Forest Service) — or start your wilderness trip from Momyer's own trail instead, permit still required.

sections.heading: Conditions caveat
sections.body: This canyon has a history: a September 2025 debris-flow event closed Highway 38 and blocked Valley of the Falls Drive for a time (fully reopened — Caltrans shows no restrictions as of July 2026), and an annually-renewed closure has applied to the area above the Big Falls viewing platform in recent years. Check the San Bernardino NF alerts page and Caltrans SR-38 conditions before a storm-season trip.

faq.q: Do you need a permit to day-hike Vivian Creek or San Gorgonio?
faq.a: Yes — a free San Gorgonio Wilderness permit is required for all day hikes and overnights, issued online only at permits.sgwa.org (released 90 days ahead). There is no in-person issuance, and rangers check on the trail.

faq.q: Where do you park for Big Falls waterfall?
faq.a: The same Falls Picnic Area lot on Valley of the Falls Drive that Vivian Creek hikers use, with the same Adventure Pass requirement. The Big Falls Trail itself is a short 0.3-mile walk from the lot.

faq.q: What if the Vivian Creek lot is full?
faq.a: The site closes when full, and roadside parking on Valley of the Falls Drive is prohibited. The official overflow is Momyer Trailhead, about 3 miles back down the drive — free to park, no Adventure Pass required — followed by an uphill road walk to the picnic area.

faq.q: Do you need an Adventure Pass at Momyer Trailhead?
faq.a: No — Momyer has no parking fee, unlike the Falls Picnic Area. You still need the free SGWA wilderness permit to hike into the San Gorgonio Wilderness from there.

properties.name: East Fork Trailhead (Bridge to Nowhere)
properties.meta_title: East Fork Trailhead Parking (Bridge to Nowhere): Buy the Pass First (2026)
properties.meta_description: Parking for the Bridge to Nowhere at the East Fork Day Use Trailhead on Camp Bonita Road: Adventure Pass required and not sold at the lot, limited designated spaces, a vault toilet, open 24 hours — and hikers reporting 5:30am arrivals on summer weekends.
properties.card_summary: Open 24 hours, vault toilet, Adventure Pass required — and not sold anywhere near the lot. Hikers report arriving before 8am on weekends.
properties.quick_answer: Buy the pass before you leave town. The East Fork Day Use Parking Trailhead at the end of Camp Bonita Road needs an Adventure Pass ($5/day, $30/year, or an Interagency pass) and there is no vendor at the trailhead — the nearest Forest Service fee station is back down the canyon and weekends-only, cash only. The lot is open 24 hours with a vault toilet and no potable water, the Forest Service says flatly that space is limited, and hiker reports on summer weekends describe arrivals well before 8am.

badges.label: Pass required, not sold on site

properties.fill_time: Summer and spring weekends: hiker reports describe arriving before 8am, some as early as 5:30am
properties.overflow: Cars spill onto the approach road — a pass still has to be displayed, and USFS says don't block roadways, gates, or dumpsters
properties.tow_risk: Citations for unpassed vehicles and for blocking the road, gates, or the dumpster area

sections.heading: The pass is the trap
sections.body: Almost everything about this trailhead is forgiving — open 24 hours, a vault toilet, a paved drive right to the end of Camp Bonita Road — except the one thing people leave to chance. An Adventure Pass or Interagency pass has to be on the dash, and nothing at the trailhead sells one. The Forest Service's San Gabriel Canyon Gateway Info Site / fee station at 15200 N San Gabriel Canyon Road in Azusa sells them, but it's weekends only, roughly 8am–4pm, cash. Miss that window and you're driving back to a sporting-goods store in the valley. On spaces, we'll say what the Forest Service says and nothing more: parking is limited, park only in designated spots, and don't block roadways, gates, or dumpsters. No agency publishes a capacity number for this lot, so we won't invent one. What hikers consistently report is the shape of the problem — the designated spaces go early on warm weekends, and 5:30am arrivals are not unusual for the Bridge to Nowhere crowd.

sections.heading: The permit question, honestly
sections.body: The Forest Service contradicts itself here and it's worth knowing before a ranger asks. The East Fork Trail page says a wilderness permit is required if you enter at the East Fork Trailhead. The East Fork Day Use Parking Trailhead page says a permit is encouraged but strictly voluntary at the same spot. Either way it's free and it takes thirty seconds: self-register at the kiosk at the trailhead. Fill one out. It costs you nothing and resolves the disagreement in your favor. The San Gabriel Mountains National Monument office at (626) 335-1251 is the number if you want it settled definitively.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: There is no sanctioned overflow lot. When the designated spaces are gone, cars line the approach road — that still requires a displayed pass, and the Forest Service's own instruction is to keep roadways, gates, and the dumpster area clear. Blocking any of those is how a long river day ends with a citation. The real plan B is temporal, not spatial: this is a hot, exposed, river-bottom hike that most people start at dawn anyway. Come on a weekday, or start before sunrise. If you arrive at 10am on a July Saturday to a full lot, the honest move is to drive twenty minutes to another canyon rather than circle. And skip it entirely after rain — the first three miles follow the streambed with repeated crossings, and swift water here has killed people.

faq.q: Do you need an Adventure Pass to park for the Bridge to Nowhere?
faq.a: Yes. The East Fork Day Use Parking Trailhead is a Forest Service fee site: $5 per vehicle per day or $30 per year, or an America the Beautiful Interagency pass. Passes are not sold at the trailhead — buy one before you drive up.

faq.q: Where can you buy an Adventure Pass near the East Fork trailhead?
faq.a: The Forest Service's San Gabriel Canyon fee station at 15200 N San Gabriel Canyon Road in Azusa sells them, but it operates weekends only (roughly 8am–4pm) and is cash only. Otherwise buy online or from a valley vendor before you go.

faq.q: Do you need a wilderness permit for the East Fork Trail?
faq.a: Forest Service pages disagree — the trail page calls it required for entry at the East Fork Trailhead, the parking-area page calls it voluntary. It's free and self-issued at the trailhead kiosk, so fill one out regardless.

faq.q: What time does the East Fork trailhead parking fill up?
faq.a: The lot is open 24 hours, but hiker reports on warm-season weekends describe designated spaces filling before 8am, with some arriving around 5:30am. Weekdays are dramatically easier.

properties.name: Chantry Flat (Big Santa Anita Canyon)
properties.meta_title: Chantry Flat Parking 2026: Open Again, Gate 6am–8pm, Fills at Dawn
properties.meta_description: Chantry Flat is open in 2026 after the Bobcat and Eaton Fire closures — but the gate runs 6am–8pm, the lots fill before sunrise on weekends, road parking is ticketed if you're over the white line, and the Mt. Wilson side is still inside a Forest Service closure order.
properties.card_summary: Open again after two fire closures. Gate 6am–8pm, lots gone before sunrise on weekends, and the Mt. Wilson side is still closed through 2027.
properties.quick_answer: Yes, it's open — and that's the recent news, not the permanent state. Chantry Flat reopened in October 2024 after the Bobcat Fire, closed again within months for the January 2025 Eaton Fire, and reopened in April 2025. In July 2026 the road is open, the gate runs 6am–8pm, and an Adventure Pass ($5/day, $30/year) is required. The lots fill before sunrise on weekends. Two catches: the Forest Service warns the road may close for safety at any time, and the Mt. Wilson side of the canyon is still inside the Eaton Fire Area Closure through December 31, 2027.

badges.label: Open — but road can close anytime

sections.heading: Open — with an asterisk you should actually read
sections.body: Big Santa Anita Canyon spent four years mostly shut. The 2020 Bobcat Fire closed it; the road repairs finally let it reopen in October 2024; the Eaton Fire in January 2025 slammed it shut again within three months; the Forest Service reopened Chantry Flat along with several other trails in April 2025. As of July 2026 it's open, and hikers report Sturtevant Falls running. The asterisk is in the Forest Service's own language: the roadway into Chantry Flat "may be closed to vehicles for safety reasons at any time in this steep environment, even if the picnic area and adjacent hiking trails are open to the public to hike in." That isn't boilerplate — the road is managed by three different entities: the City of Sierra Madre controls the gate, LA County maintains most of the pavement, and the Forest Service owns a short section. Repairs and storm damage can shut the drive with no notice and no coordination with the trail status. Check the official Chantry Flat page or call the LA Gateway District at (818) 899-1900 the morning you go.

sections.heading: Parking: the gate is the whole game
sections.body: Day-use hours are 6am to 8pm, and on weekends the queue forms at the gate before it opens. There are upper and lower paved lots at the flat plus road spaces on the way up; no agency publishes a space count and we're not going to guess one. What's consistent across every source is that they're all gone early on weekend mornings — this is the closest real waterfall hike to a metro area of thirteen million people, reached by one two-lane road. If you park on the road below the lots, the rule is specific and enforced: your car must be entirely behind the white line or the Forest Service will ticket it, and anything narrowing the road gets towed. An Adventure Pass or Interagency pass goes on the dash wherever you end up. On the plus side, this is a genuinely developed site — flush toilets and drinking fountains at both lots, picnic tables and grills.

sections.heading: What's still closed up here
sections.body: Don't plan a Mt. Wilson summit from this canyon without checking first. The Eaton Fire Area Closure (Forest Order 05-01-26-04) runs through December 31, 2027 and covers the Mount Wilson Toll Road, Idlehour, Castle Canyon, the Sam Merrill trails, Echo Mountain, and both Mount Lowe trails. Separately — and this trips people up because of the name — the Mt. Wilson Trail is not a Chantry Flat trail at all. It starts in Sierra Madre off Mira Monte Avenue, miles away, with its own street parking. Volunteers rebuilt 24 damaged sections after the Eaton Fire and the lower ~3.5 miles reopened in time for the Mount Wilson Trail Race to return in May 2026, but the upper trail remains inside the closure. A hiker died on the reopened lower section in May 2026 after a fall near the fixed-rope stretch; it is not the casual walk the reopening headlines implied.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: Three real options when you arrive to a full flat. One: park on the road below, entirely behind the white line, and walk up. Two: Adams Pack Station at the flat sells paid parking — reported around $25 — which also buys you a snack bar and the resident donkeys. Three: leave. There's no third lot, and circling a full canyon road with a gate at the bottom is how people burn an hour. If the gate is shut when you get there, don't improvise on the residential streets of Arcadia and Sierra Madre below — head for Switzer Falls instead, which is about forty minutes west and serves the same waterfall itch from a different canyon.

faq.q: Is Chantry Flat open in 2026?
faq.a: Yes. It reopened in April 2025 after the Eaton Fire closure, and the road and picnic area are open as of July 2026 with day-use hours of 6am to 8pm. The Forest Service cautions the road may close for safety at any time, so confirm before driving up.

faq.q: What time do you need to arrive at Chantry Flat to get parking?
faq.a: On weekends, before the 6am gate opening — hikers routinely queue at the gate. Weekdays are far easier. There's no published space count, but every lot at the flat fills early on busy mornings.

faq.q: Can you park on the road at Chantry Flat?
faq.a: Yes, below the lots, but your vehicle must be entirely behind the white line or the Forest Service will cite it, and anything blocking the road gets towed. An Adventure Pass is still required.

faq.q: Is Sturtevant Falls open?
faq.a: Yes — Sturtevant Falls, the Sturtevant Trail and Loop, First Water, the Gabrielino Trail, and the Upper and Lower Winter Creek trails have been reported open in 2026. The Mount Wilson Toll Road and the Eaton Fire closure area to the west remain closed through December 31, 2027.

faq.q: Do you need an Adventure Pass at Chantry Flat?
faq.a: Yes — $5 per vehicle per day or $30 per year, with America the Beautiful Interagency passes also accepted. It applies in the lots and on the road spaces alike.

related.label: Switzer Falls — the other front-range waterfall lot

properties.name: Switzer Picnic Area (Switzer Falls)
properties.meta_title: Switzer Falls Parking 2026: Two Lots, a Gated Spur Road & a 7am Deadline
properties.meta_description: Switzer Picnic Area parking on Angeles Crest Highway: a small upper lot at the highway and a larger lower lot down a gated spur road. Adventure Pass required, vault toilets, no water — and hikers say be there before 7am on weekends. Hwy 2 access from La Cañada is open.
properties.card_summary: Small upper lot at the highway, bigger lower lot down a gated spur. Be there before 7am on weekends or you're walking the spur road.
properties.quick_answer: Two lots and a gate decide your day. A small lot at Angeles Crest Highway (mile marker 34.2) and a larger lot a quarter-mile down a steep gated spur road. Day use is 6am–10pm, an Adventure Pass ($5/day, $30/year) or Interagency pass is required, and there are vault toilets but no potable water. Hikers say to be there before 7am on weekends for the lower lot — otherwise you park up top and walk the spur down, then back up at the end.

properties.fee: Adventure Pass $5/day or $30/year; Interagency passes accepted
properties.lot_size: Small upper lot at the highway plus a larger lower lot by the picnic area (26 picnic sites, one ADA space); no official car count published
properties.fill_time: Weekends: lower lot before 7am, upper lots not long after
properties.overflow: The upper lots at the highway — costs you a steep half-mile down and back up
properties.tow_risk: Angeles Crest shoulders are narrow and patrolled; park in the designated lots
properties.road_access: Paved: SR-2 (Angeles Crest Hwy) ~10 mi north of the 210 in La Cañada Flintridge; spur road to the lower lot is gated at the top

properties.address: Switzer Picnic Area, Angeles Crest Highway (SR-2), mile marker 34.2, La Cañada Flintridge, CA 91011

sections.heading: Highway 2 access: fine, if you're coming from La Cañada
sections.body: Switzer sits at mile marker 34.2, about ten miles up Angeles Crest Highway from the 210 in La Cañada Flintridge — and that stretch is open. The Highway 2 closures people have been reading about since the storm damage are far east of here: Caltrans lists SR-2 closed from 3.3 miles east of Newcomb's Ranch to Islip Saddle and again from Islip Saddle to Big Pines for emergency repairs, roughly forty repair sites in all. What that means practically: you can drive to Switzer normally from the west, but you cannot drive through to Wrightwood or the east end of the range. If your plan involved approaching from Big Pines, it doesn't work. Check Caltrans SR-2 conditions before any Angeles Crest trip — this highway's status changes every storm season.

sections.heading: The two-lot problem
sections.body: Switzer has a small parking lot right at the Crest Highway and a larger one about a quarter-mile down a steep spur road, with a gate at the top of the spur. The lower lot is the one everybody wants — it puts you at the picnic area and the Gabrielino Trail with no road walk. It's also the one that's gone first, and hiker reports converge on the same advice: be there before 7am on weekends, or expect to circle while people wait for someone to leave. The Forest Service publishes 26 picnic sites and one accessible parking space but no vehicle capacity, so we won't quote a number. Facilities are real if basic: vault toilets, no potable water — Arroyo Seco Creek water is seasonal and needs treatment, so carry what you'll drink. Day use runs 6am to 10pm per the Forest Service. One caveat worth knowing: longtime local references describe the spur-road gate closing earlier in the evening than the posted day-use window, which would strand a car in the lower lot. If you're planning a late finish, park up top.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: The good news about Switzer is that plan B is built in. When the lower lot is full, park in the upper lot at the highway and walk down the spur — about half a mile, steep enough that you'll notice it on the way back out, but it's pavement and it's legal. That's the whole overflow strategy, and it's why Switzer handles crowds better than most front-range trailheads. What isn't a plan: improvising on the Angeles Crest shoulders. They're narrow, they're patrolled, and there are proper lots fifty yards away. If everything's full and you'd rather not do the road walk, Chantry Flat is the other big front-range waterfall option about forty minutes east — though its gate closes when it fills, so call ahead rather than driving on hope.

faq.q: Do you need an Adventure Pass to park at Switzer Falls?
faq.a: Yes. Switzer Picnic Area is a Forest Service fee site: $5 per vehicle per day or $30 per year, or an America the Beautiful Interagency pass, displayed on the dash.

faq.q: What time should you arrive at Switzer Picnic Area on a weekend?
faq.a: Before 7am if you want the larger lower lot by the picnic area. After that you'll typically be parking in the smaller upper lot at Angeles Crest Highway and walking about half a mile down the steep spur road — and back up at the end.

faq.q: Is Angeles Crest Highway open to Switzer Falls in 2026?
faq.a: Yes. Switzer is at mile marker 34.2, about ten miles up SR-2 from La Cañada Flintridge, and that section is open. The current Caltrans closures for emergency repairs are far east — from 3.3 miles east of Newcomb's Ranch to Islip Saddle, and from Islip Saddle to Big Pines — so you cannot drive through the range.

faq.q: Are there restrooms at the Switzer trailhead?
faq.a: Vault toilets, yes. Potable water, no — Arroyo Seco Creek flow is seasonal and would need treatment, so bring all the water you plan to drink.

properties.name: Icehouse Canyon Trailhead (Mt. Baldy)
properties.meta_title: Icehouse Canyon Parking: Adventure Pass vs. Cucamonga Permit (2026)
properties.meta_description: Icehouse Canyon trailhead parking on Ice House Canyon Road: Adventure Pass required to park, plus a separate free wilderness permit to enter the Cucamonga Wilderness — self-issue at the trailhead for the Angeles side, a different permit for the San Bernardino side. Lot fills early.
properties.card_summary: Two pieces of paper, two agencies. Adventure Pass to park, free wilderness permit to hike — and which permit depends on which peak.
properties.quick_answer: The pass and the permit are different things, and you need both. Parking at the Icehouse Canyon lot on Ice House Canyon Road is a Forest Service fee site: $5 per vehicle per day via an Adventure Pass or Interagency pass. Entering the Cucamonga Wilderness beyond the boundary needs a separate, free wilderness permit — self-issue at the brown box in the parking lot for the Angeles side (Icehouse Saddle, Ontario Peak, the Three T's), but a different permit for the San Bernardino side (Cucamonga and Etiwanda Peaks). The Forest Service says parking is limited, especially on weekends.
properties.trail_served: Icehouse Canyon Trail 7W07 to Icehouse Saddle (7.2 mi RT, ~2,600 ft gain, high point 7,632 ft); onward to Ontario Peak, the Three T's, Cucamonga Peak, Etiwanda Peak
properties.fee: $5/vehicle/day Adventure Pass ($30/year); Interagency passes accepted. Wilderness permit is free and separate
properties.lot_size: Trailhead lot on Ice House Canyon Road; Forest Service publishes no count and says parking is limited
properties.fill_time: Weekends: early — hikers report arriving around 6am even on weekdays for a comfortable spot
properties.overflow: Cars line Ice House Canyon Road and Mt. Baldy Road; this is a cabin community, so driveways and private frontage are genuinely private
properties.tow_risk: Blocking cabin driveways or narrowing the canyon road is the way to get towed here
properties.restrooms: Not confirmed — the Forest Service lists no restroom information for this site; plan for none
properties.road_access: Paved: Mt. Baldy Rd through Mt. Baldy Village, then Ice House Canyon Rd; Mt. Baldy Visitor Center is a quarter-mile away, open weekends only
properties.best_season: Late spring through fall. Winter and early spring here require mountaineering skills — people die on this trail
properties.address: 7698 Ice House Canyon Road, Mt. Baldy, CA 91759

sections.heading: Adventure Pass vs. wilderness permit — they are not the same thing
sections.body: This is the single most common mistake at Icehouse Canyon, and it's an easy one to make because both are Forest Service paperwork. The Adventure Pass is a parking fee: $5 per vehicle per day or $30 per year, or an America the Beautiful Interagency pass, displayed on the dash. It buys you the space and nothing else. The Cucamonga Wilderness permit is free and is about entering the wilderness, not parking. Having one does not park your car; having a pass does not let you cross the boundary. You need both, and rangers do check.

sections.heading: Which permit, though — and this is where it gets messy
sections.body: The Cucamonga Wilderness straddles two national forests, and each runs its own system. Staying in the Angeles National Forest — Icehouse Saddle, Ontario Peak, the Three T's — is the easy case: a self-issue permit from the brown box in the Icehouse Canyon parking lot, available whenever you show up. The Mt. Baldy Visitor Center a quarter-mile away also issues them, but it's weekends only. Continuing into the San Bernardino National Forest — Cucamonga Peak, Etiwanda Peak, the interior — requires a separate free permit, and the guidance is genuinely inconsistent. The San Bernardino NF's Cucamonga Wilderness page routes permits through the San Gorgonio Wilderness Association, requestable up to 90 days ahead and issued online rather than in person. Other current guidance points hikers to the Lytle Creek Ranger Station at (909) 382-2851 or an online application. If Cucamonga Peak is your objective, sort this out days before, not at the trailhead box — the brown box does not cover you past the forest boundary.

sections.heading: Parking and the seasonal closure nobody expects
sections.body: The Forest Service's line on the lot is short: "Parking is limited, especially on weekends. Carpooling is recommended." No capacity is published, so we won't put a number on it. What's clear from hiker reports is that it goes early — people describe 6am arrivals on weekdays for a comfortable spot. Overflow spills along Ice House Canyon Road and Mt. Baldy Road, and it matters here more than most places that this is a cabin community: those driveways belong to people who live there. The other surprise is closures. The 2024 Bridge Fire shut this whole area, and that closure order was terminated in June 2025 — but the Angeles has since issued late-winter safety closures of the Mt. Baldy-area trails, Icehouse Canyon Trail 7W07 included, most recently for a stretch in February–March 2026, with trails reported open again by April. The Forest Service is blunt about why: serious injuries and deaths have occurred on the Icehouse Canyon and Mt. Baldy trails, and hiking here in winter, spring, or even early summer "requires experienced mountaineering skills." Check the Angeles NF alerts page before a cold-season attempt.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: If the lot is full, the honest options are roadside parking on Ice House Canyon Road or Mt. Baldy Road — legal where it doesn't block a driveway or narrow the road, still requiring a displayed pass — or driving on. There is no second Icehouse lot. The better plan B is upstream of the drive: make it a weekday, or start at first light. And if your permit situation for Cucamonga Peak fell through, the Angeles-side objectives from the same trailhead — Icehouse Saddle, Ontario Peak, the Three T's — are covered by the self-issue box in the lot, so the day doesn't have to end at the parking area.

faq.q: Do you need an Adventure Pass at Icehouse Canyon?
faq.a: Yes — the trailhead lot is a Forest Service fee site at $5 per vehicle per day or $30 per year, with America the Beautiful Interagency passes accepted. It covers parking only, not wilderness entry.

faq.q: What's the difference between the Adventure Pass and the Cucamonga Wilderness permit?
faq.a: The Adventure Pass is a paid parking fee for the lot. The Cucamonga Wilderness permit is free and authorizes entering the wilderness area itself. They are separate requirements and you need both — one does not substitute for the other.

faq.q: Where do you get a Cucamonga Wilderness permit?
faq.a: For destinations within the Angeles National Forest — Icehouse Saddle, Ontario Peak, the Three T's — self-issue at the brown box in the Icehouse Canyon parking lot, or at the Mt. Baldy Visitor Center a quarter-mile away (weekends only). For Cucamonga Peak, Etiwanda Peak, and the San Bernardino National Forest portion, you need a separate free permit obtained in advance, either through the San Gorgonio Wilderness Association's online system or via the Lytle Creek Ranger Station at (909) 382-2851.

faq.q: How early does Icehouse Canyon parking fill up?
faq.a: Early. The Forest Service simply says parking is limited, especially on weekends, and recommends carpooling; hikers report arriving around 6am even on weekdays to park comfortably. Overflow lines Ice House Canyon Road and Mt. Baldy Road, where cabin driveways must stay clear.

faq.q: Is the Icehouse Canyon Trail open in 2026?
faq.a: It has been open since spring 2026. The 2024 Bridge Fire closure was terminated in June 2025, but the Angeles National Forest has issued late-winter safety closures covering Icehouse Canyon Trail 7W07 — most recently a stretch in February and March 2026. Check the forest alerts page before any cold-season trip.

properties.name: Cedar Creek Falls Trailhead (San Diego River Gorge)
properties.meta_title: Cedar Creek Falls Parking (Ramona): Closure Status & the $6 Permit Trap (2026)
properties.meta_description: Closed under heat order #02-26-14 as of our July 20, 2026 check — verify before you drive. When open, parking at the San Diego River Gorge trailhead is free: the $6 Recreation.gov permit buys entry to the falls area, not a parking space, and isn't sold on site.
properties.card_summary: Closed under a heat order as of July 20, 2026 — check before you drive. When open: parking is free, and the $6 Recreation.gov permit buys entry to the falls area, not a parking space.
properties.quick_answer: Check the closure before anything else — this area was closed when we last looked. Forest Order #02-26-14 closed the San Diego River Gorge / Cedar Creek Falls area on July 14, 2026, and on our July 20, 2026 check it was still posted with no end date. Heat orders here come and go, so verify on the Cleveland National Forest alerts page the morning you go. When it is open, the thing people get backwards: parking at the trailhead (15519 Thornbush Rd, Ramona) is free — no Adventure Pass, no kiosk. The $6 Recreation.gov permit buys entry to the falls area, covers the holder plus up to four others, is not sold at the trailhead, and there's no usable cell signal to buy it there. Rangers check.

badges.label: Buy the permit before you drive

properties.trail_served: San Diego River Gorge Trail to Cedar Creek Falls (~6 mi RT; ~1,800 ft of climbing on the way back out)
properties.fee: Parking: free. Entry permit: $6 per permit (holder + up to 4 others) via Recreation.gov — required, not sold on site
properties.lot_size: Small — hiker reports describe roughly a dozen marked spaces; no official count published
properties.fill_time: Cool-season weekend mornings are the crunch; in summer the area is often closed outright rather than crowded
properties.overflow: None sanctioned — and with a permit cap, an overflow lot wouldn't help anyway
properties.tow_risk: Thornbush Rd is a residential street in Ramona; don't improvise on it. Citations are for missing permits, not parking
properties.road_access: Paved suburban streets through Ramona to the Thornbush Rd trailhead
properties.best_season: Fall, winter and spring. The Forest Service actively discourages this hike above 90°F
properties.address: 15519 Thornbush Rd, Ramona, CA 92065

sections.heading: The confusion that ruins people's mornings
sections.body: Two separate things get collapsed into one here. Parking at the San Diego River Gorge trailhead is free — the Forest Service's own trail page and the Recreation.gov listing both describe the $6 charge as a visitor use permit to enter the Cedar Creek Falls Permit Area, and neither lists this trailhead as an Adventure Pass fee site. Some third-party blogs tell you to display an Adventure Pass on the dash. No official source we could find says that. The reverse mistake is worse: showing up with the intention of paying at the trailhead. Permits are sold online only, at recreation.gov/permits/234735, and there is no meaningful cell signal at the trailhead to buy one. Book it at home, screenshot or print it, and expect a ranger to ask. $6 covers the permit holder plus up to four more people — it's per permit, not per person, and not per car.

sections.heading: Summer is the real gatekeeper
sections.body: This canyon has killed people. The trail drops about 1,800 feet in the first two miles, which means the hard part is the exit, in full sun, in the afternoon. The Forest Service's guidance is blunt: hiking is highly discouraged when the trailhead is above 90°F, and the District Ranger issues temporary closure orders for the duration of high-heat events — when that happens, Recreation.gov cancels affected reservations with a full refund. That isn't hypothetical. Forest Order #02-26-14 closed the San Diego River Gorge / Cedar Creek Falls area effective July 14, 2026, and as of this page's July 20, 2026 check the order was still posted on the Cleveland National Forest alerts page with no end date listed. Recreation.gov was also flagging that water levels had dwindled and the pools were stagnant. Summer here is not a season with bad parking; it's a season with no access. Check Cleveland NF alerts before you buy a permit, and call the Palomar Ranger District at (760) 788-0250 if you want it confirmed by a human.

sections.heading: The lot itself
sections.body: Small, at the end of a residential street in Ramona, with a toilet just above the parking area and a water spigot behind it. No official capacity is published, so we won't invent one — hikers consistently describe roughly a dozen marked spaces, which is modest but not the binding constraint. The daily permit cap is. Reporting from the launch of the reservation system, and hiking guides since, put it at 75 permits per day; the Forest Service's current public pages state the $6 price without restating the cap, so treat 75 as well-sourced but unconfirmed on the official page. Practically: if you hold a permit for the day, a space will almost certainly exist. If you don't hold a permit, an empty space does you no good.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: If the area is closed for heat, or the day's permits are gone, don't drive out and hope. Three Sisters Falls is the closest same-flavor alternative — same forest, same waterfall payoff, no permit and no fee, and a rebuilt trailhead with far more parking. It carries the identical summer heat problem and its own closure orders, so check the same alerts page. If you want a genuinely heat-proof backup, go coastal instead: Torrey Pines is 90 minutes west and about thirty degrees cooler. And if the goal was really "a hard San Diego County climb," Mt. Woodson is closer to town with no permit at all.

faq.q: Do you have to pay to park at Cedar Creek Falls?
faq.a: No. Parking at the San Diego River Gorge trailhead on Thornbush Road in Ramona is free. The $6 charge people associate with Cedar Creek Falls is a Recreation.gov visitor-use permit to enter the falls area — a separate thing from parking, and required whether or not you drove.

faq.q: How much is the Cedar Creek Falls permit and what does it cover?
faq.a: $6 per permit, which covers the permit holder plus up to four additional people (five total). It is purchased online at recreation.gov, is not sold at the trailhead, and does not include or substitute for anything parking-related. Rangers check permits on the trail.

faq.q: Do you need an Adventure Pass at Cedar Creek Falls?
faq.a: No official Forest Service or Recreation.gov source lists the San Diego River Gorge trailhead as an Adventure Pass fee site — the required document is the $6 Recreation.gov permit. Some third-party guides claim otherwise; the official sources do not.

faq.q: Is Cedar Creek Falls open in summer?
faq.a: Often not. The Cleveland National Forest closes the San Diego River Gorge / Cedar Creek Falls area during high-heat events, and such an order was in effect from July 14, 2026. The falls also typically stop flowing in summer, leaving stagnant, algae-filled pools. Fall through spring is the season to go.

faq.q: Can you buy the Cedar Creek Falls permit at the trailhead?
faq.a: No. Permits are online-only through Recreation.gov, and there is not enough cell service at the trailhead to buy one on your phone. Book before you leave home and bring a printout or screenshot.
