properties.name: Three Sisters Falls (Cha'chaany Hamuk Trailhead)
properties.meta_title: Three Sisters Falls Parking: Check the Heat Closure First (2026)
properties.meta_description: The rebuilt Cha'chaany Hamuk Trailhead gave Three Sisters Falls hardpacked parking for up to 80 vehicles, vault toilets and shade — free, no permit. The catch is closure orders: Cleveland NF shuts this trail during high-heat events, most recently July 14–16, 2026.
properties.card_summary: Free parking for up to 80 cars at the rebuilt trailhead, no permit needed — but the Forest Service closes this trail outright during heat events. Check alerts before you drive.
properties.quick_answer: Check the closure page before anything else. Cleveland National Forest shuts this trail during high-heat events — Forest Order #02-26-13 closed the Three Sisters area July 14–16, 2026, and it had expired as of this July 20 check, but forest-wide fire restrictions (#02-26-10) remain in effect with fire danger rated High. If it's open, parking is the easy part now: the rebuilt Cha'chaany Hamuk Trailhead has hardpacked parking for up to 80 vehicles, vault toilets, shade structures and picnic tables — free, no permit, no Adventure Pass. Getting there is the remaining chore: about 13 miles of washboarded Boulder Creek Road.
properties.trail_served: Three Sisters Falls Trail (moderate–strenuous, rocky and unstable in places, essentially no shade); also the approach used for Eagle Peak
properties.fee: Free — no fee, no permit, no Adventure Pass
properties.lot_size: Hardpacked parking for up to 80 vehicles including accessible spaces (per the National Forest Foundation project description)
properties.fill_time: Rarely the binding constraint since the rebuild — the trailhead was built to absorb weekends that draw 50–400 visitors
properties.overflow: Use the lot. Roadside parking on Boulder Creek Road was the exact problem this project was built to end
properties.tow_risk: Parking along the edge of Boulder Creek Road blocks residents and emergency vehicles and is not tolerated — there is no reason to do it now
properties.road_access: Boulder Creek Road: ~13 miles of gravel and washboard from Descanso. Passable for low-clearance 2WD at slow speeds
properties.best_season: Late fall through spring. Summer here is a rescue statistic, not a hike
properties.address: Cha'chaany Hamuk Trailhead, Boulder Creek Rd, between Descanso and Santa Ysabel, CA

sections.heading: Access status comes before parking
sections.body: This trail's limiting factor isn't spaces, it's whether the forest is letting anyone in. The Palomar Ranger District issues short, sharp closure orders when the National Weather Service posts excessive-heat warnings: Forest Order #02-26-13 prohibited being upon the Three Sisters Closure Area from July 14 through July 16, 2026. That window had lapsed by our July 20 check, and no successor Three Sisters order was posted — but the neighboring Cedar Creek Falls closure (#02-26-14) was still up with no end date, and elevated fire-use restrictions (#02-26-10, effective June 11, 2026) apply forest-wide with fire danger listed as High across all three districts. So: check the Cleveland NF alerts page the morning you go, and call the Palomar Ranger District at (760) 788-0250 if the page is ambiguous. It is a long drive on a bad road to find a gate.

sections.heading: The trailhead is genuinely fixed now
sections.body: For years this was a roadside free-for-all: cars strung along the shoulder of Boulder Creek Road on weekends that draw anywhere from 50 to 400 visitors, narrowing the road for residents and emergency vehicles. A roughly $1.57-million reconstruction, delivered with the National Forest Foundation and opened with a ribbon-cutting in May, replaced it with the Cha'chaany Hamuk Trailhead — the old Three Sisters Falls Trailhead, renamed. What's there: hardpacked parking for up to 80 vehicles, including accessible spaces, four vault toilet rooms, trash and recycling, accessible picnic tables, shade structures and interpretive signage — plus a helicopter landing zone and underground water storage for wildfire response, which tells you something about how this canyon behaves. It's all free. No permit, no fee, no Adventure Pass; unlike Cedar Creek Falls, there is nothing to book. Older trail guides still describe "no facilities" and a rutted dirt lot. They're out of date.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: If the trail is closed for heat, the honest plan B is not another backcountry waterfall — it's leaving the backcountry. Cedar Creek Falls sits in the same watershed and gets closed by the same weather, and it adds a permit you'd have to buy in advance. When the Palomar district is in a heat closure, assume both are out. Go coastal instead — Torrey Pines — or head to Mt. Woodson, which is lower, closer to help, and served by a park with water and shade at the bottom. If the trail is open but you're worried about the drive, note that Boulder Creek Road is graded gravel rather than four-wheel-drive terrain: slow is sufficient, high clearance is not required.

faq.q: Is Three Sisters Falls open right now?
faq.a: It depends on the weather. Cleveland National Forest issues temporary closure orders for the Three Sisters area during excessive-heat events — Forest Order #02-26-13 closed it July 14–16, 2026. Check the Cleveland NF alerts page or call the Palomar Ranger District at (760) 788-0250 before driving out.

faq.q: Do you need a permit or a fee to hike Three Sisters Falls?
faq.a: No. Unlike nearby Cedar Creek Falls, Three Sisters Falls requires no permit and charges no parking fee, and it is not an Adventure Pass site. Access is free when the area is open.

faq.q: How big is the Three Sisters Falls parking lot?
faq.a: The rebuilt Cha'chaany Hamuk Trailhead has hardpacked parking for up to 80 vehicles, including accessible spaces, according to the National Forest Foundation, which partnered on the project. It also has vault toilets, shade structures and picnic tables.

faq.q: Can you park on Boulder Creek Road for Three Sisters Falls?
faq.a: Don't. Illegal roadside parking along Boulder Creek Road restricted resident and emergency-vehicle access and was the specific problem the new 80-vehicle trailhead was built to solve. Use the lot.

faq.q: What kind of car do you need for Boulder Creek Road?
faq.a: A normal one, driven slowly. Boulder Creek Road is roughly 13 miles of gravel with heavily washboarded sections from Descanso, but it is passable for low-clearance two-wheel-drive vehicles.

related.label: Cedar Creek Falls — same watershed, but you need a permit

properties.name: Potato Chip Rock / Mt. Woodson (Lake Poway)
properties.meta_title: Potato Chip Rock Parking: Lake Poway's $10 Weekend Fee vs. Free Hwy 67 (2026)
properties.meta_description: Lake Poway charges non-residents $10 to park on weekends and holidays only — weekdays are free for everyone. The Hwy 67 roadside approach is always free and half the distance, but it's a shadeless paved climb beside a fast four-lane highway.
properties.card_summary: Two trailheads, two different deals: Lake Poway's big lot ($10 non-resident weekends/holidays, free weekdays, gates locked at sunset) or free roadside on Hwy 67 for a shorter, uglier, steeper climb.
properties.quick_answer: The fee is narrower than people think. Lake Poway charges $10 for non-residents on weekends and holidays only — collected 6am to 30 minutes before sunset. Weekdays are free for everyone, and Poway residents with proof, active military with ID, and valid fishing-permit holders are always free. The alternative is the Hwy 67 trailhead: free roadside parking, about half the round-trip distance, but a steep paved fire road with no shade and no facilities, parked beside a fast four-lane highway. Neither is an Adventure Pass site — this is a city park and county open space, not national forest. Lake Poway's gates are locked at sunset.
properties.trail_served: Mt. Woodson Trail to Potato Chip Rock and the summit — ~7 mi RT from Lake Poway, ~4 mi RT from Hwy 67
properties.fee: Lake Poway: $10 non-resident, weekends and holidays only; free weekdays; free for Poway residents, active military with ID, and fishing-permit holders. Hwy 67: free
properties.lot_size: Lake Poway: a large park lot with restrooms and water. Hwy 67: informal roadside, no official count
properties.fill_time: The lot rarely runs out before the summit-photo line does — hikers routinely advise arriving before 9am on weekends to avoid a 30-plus-minute queue at the rock
properties.overflow: The Hwy 67 trailhead is the de facto overflow, and it's free — but it's a different, harder-feeling hike
properties.tow_risk: On Hwy 67, park fully clear of the travel lanes and never block the road into the fire station. At Lake Poway the risk isn't towing — it's being locked inside after sunset
properties.road_access: Lake Poway: paved city street to the entry station at the end of Lake Poway Rd. Hwy 67: paved highway shoulder
properties.best_season: October–April. This is an exposed granite climb with almost no shade — summer afternoons are miserable and occasionally dangerous
properties.address: Lake Poway, 14644 Lake Poway Rd, Poway, CA 92064

sections.heading: Lake Poway: read the fee schedule properly
sections.body: The City of Poway's own parking page is short and worth getting right, because the internet flattens it into "$10 to park at Lake Poway." What it actually says: Poway residents — no charge. Non-residents — $10. Military with ID — no charge. And the fee applies to non-residents on weekends and holidays only, collected from 6am to 30 minutes before sunset. Anyone holding a valid fishing permit is exempt. Residents can get a sticker at the entry station or office. So a Tuesday hike from Lake Poway costs nothing, for anybody. That single fact reshuffles the whole calculation below. The park itself is the comfortable option: a large lot, restrooms, water, picnic areas, and the Mt. Woodson trailhead a short walk from the far end. Gates open at 6am and are closed and locked at sunset — on a seven-mile round trip with a photo queue at the top, that's a real deadline, not a formality.

sections.heading: Hwy 67: free, shorter, and less pleasant
sections.body: The east-side approach starts from the shoulder of Highway 67 near the CAL FIRE station and follows the paved Mt. Woodson Road service road to the summit — roughly four miles round trip instead of seven, with a steeper average grade. Parking is free roadside, with no lot, no toilets, no water and no shade. The honest downsides: you are parking and stepping out onto the verge of a fast four-lane highway, the walk itself is asphalt with utility infrastructure rather than trail, and the area immediately around the fire station access road is signed and must be kept clear. Hikers consistently call it the more dangerous trailhead of the two, and that's about the cars, not the climb. It earns its keep in exactly two situations: a weekend when you don't want to pay $10, or an afternoon when you want the summit without three hours of exposure.

sections.heading: What no pass covers here
sections.body: Worth stating plainly because people arrive with the wrong paperwork: this is not national forest. Lake Poway is a City of Poway recreation area and Mt. Woodson's slopes are open space, so an Adventure Pass buys you nothing, and neither does an America the Beautiful pass or a California State Parks pass. The $10 is a municipal parking fee paid at the entry station, cash-simple and only on the days listed above.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: In order of usefulness: move the day, not the trailhead. A weekday at Lake Poway is free and quiet, and the summit-photo queue — the actual bottleneck on this hike, not parking — largely evaporates. If you're locked into a weekend, arriving before 9am gets you up and down ahead of the line. If you won't pay and can't move the day, take Hwy 67 and accept the tradeoffs. If you're chasing a summit rather than that specific photograph, Three Sisters Falls and Eagle Peak are an hour east with free parking and far fewer people — subject to the Cleveland National Forest's summer heat closures. And if the day has already gotten hot, go west: Torrey Pines is coastal, shaded by ocean air rather than trees, and open until sunset.

faq.q: How much does it cost to park at Lake Poway for Potato Chip Rock?
faq.a: $10 for non-residents, and only on weekends and holidays — weekdays are free for everyone. Poway residents with proof of residency, active military with ID, and holders of a valid fishing permit park free at all times. The fee is collected from 6am to 30 minutes before sunset.

faq.q: Can you hike Potato Chip Rock without paying?
faq.a: Yes, two ways. Hike on a weekday, when Lake Poway parking is free for everyone. Or start from the Highway 67 trailhead on the east side, where roadside parking is always free — a shorter, steeper paved route with no restrooms, water or shade.

faq.q: Which Potato Chip Rock trailhead is better, Lake Poway or Highway 67?
faq.a: Lake Poway is the nicer hike — about seven miles round trip on real trail, with restrooms, water and a lake to start from. Highway 67 is about four miles round trip on a steep paved service road with no facilities, parked on the shoulder of a fast four-lane highway. Lake Poway for the experience, Highway 67 for speed or to skip the weekend fee.

faq.q: What time does Lake Poway close?
faq.a: The gates open at 6am daily and are closed and locked at sunset. On a seven-mile round trip with a queue for the summit photo, plan your turnaround around that lock time — not around daylight.

faq.q: Do you need an Adventure Pass at Mt. Woodson?
faq.a: No. Lake Poway is a City of Poway park and Mt. Woodson is not national forest land, so an Adventure Pass, an America the Beautiful pass and a California State Parks pass are all irrelevant here. The only charge is Poway's $10 non-resident weekend and holiday parking fee.

related.label: Adventure Pass: why it doesn't apply here
City of Poway parking & entry station

properties.name: Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve
properties.meta_title: Torrey Pines Parking: Demand-Based Fees, Tiny Mesa Lots & the Free Street Option (2026)
properties.meta_description: Torrey Pines charges demand-based day-use parking — roughly $10–$25 per vehicle depending on lot and day — and the two small lots on the mesa fill early enough that the park road gets gated. Free street parking on N Torrey Pines Rd is real, competitive, and walkable in.
properties.card_summary: Demand-priced day use (~$10–$25), two small mesa lots that fill by mid-morning and get gated off, and free street parking outside the entrance that you have to earn by arriving early.
properties.quick_answer: The lots are small and the price moves. Torrey Pines uses demand-based day-use pricing — California State Parks lists roughly $10–$25 per vehicle depending on which lot, which day and which season — with an annual State Parks vehicle pass sold at the kiosk for $195. The two small lots up on the mesa near the Lodge and visitor center fill fast on weekends, at which point the park road is gated and a light above the entrance kiosk flags it. Free street parking exists on N Torrey Pines Rd outside the gate and walking in on foot is free — but those spaces are contested from mid-morning on. Gates open 7:15am and close at sunset. No dogs, anywhere in the reserve.
properties.trail_served: Guy Fleming, Razor Point, Yucca Point, Broken Hill and the Beach Trail down to Flat Rock — short reserve loops, not a big-mileage day
properties.fee: Demand-based day use: California State Parks lists the North Beach lot at $10–$25 and the South Beach lot from $12, up to $25 at peak. Annual State Parks vehicle pass $195 at the kiosk. Walking in is free
properties.lot_size: Two small lots on the mesa near the Lodge, plus the larger South Beach lot at the bottom of the hill and a separate North Beach lot
properties.fill_time: Mesa lots: often full by mid-morning on weekends, with hikers advising arrival before 8–9am. 10am–1pm is the reserve's busiest window
properties.overflow: South Beach lot at the base, then free street parking on N Torrey Pines Rd — the walk up the park road is the tradeoff
properties.tow_risk: Low inside the park. On the street, read the posted signs — this is city right-of-way near a state beach, not a free-for-all
properties.road_access: Paved throughout, off N Torrey Pines Rd. Note the recurring closure of the park road and upper lot the 3rd Wednesday of every month, 7am–1pm
properties.best_season: Year-round. Winter and spring are best for wildflowers and clear air; summer weekends are the parking crunch
properties.address: 12600 N Torrey Pines Rd, San Diego, CA 92037

sections.heading: Three lots, two of them tiny
sections.body: Torrey Pines is not one parking area, it's a hierarchy. At the bottom of the hill, just inside the entrance off N Torrey Pines Rd, is the South Beach lot — the big one, with restrooms. Drive up the park road and you reach two small lots on the mesa near the Lodge, the visitor center and the trailheads, which is where everyone actually wants to be. Separately, off Carmel Valley Rd, is the North Beach lot, which has its own fee schedule. The mesa lots are the story. They hold very few cars relative to demand, and when they're full the park road is closed to vehicles at the bottom — a flashing light above the entrance kiosk signals it so you're not queuing for something that isn't available. On weekends that happens early; the common advice is to be through the gate before 8 or 9am, and the reserve's own busiest window is roughly 10am to 1pm. Arrive at 11am on a July Saturday and the realistic outcome is the South Beach lot and a walk up the hill, which is a perfectly good warm-up and not what most people planned. One calendar item worth noting: California State Parks lists a recurring park road and upper parking lot closure on the 3rd Wednesday of every month, 7am–1pm.

sections.heading: What it costs, and what pass actually works
sections.body: Day use here is demand-based, which means the number on the sign changes with the day, the season and the holiday calendar. California State Parks publishes the North Beach lot at $10–$25 per vehicle and the South Beach lot starting at $12 and running to the same ceiling; expect the top of the range on summer weekends and holidays. Payment is at automated machines or the kiosk when it's staffed. An annual California State Parks Vehicle Day Use Pass is $195 at the entrance kiosk and covers it. What does not work: an Adventure Pass. This is a state reserve, not national forest — the Adventure Pass and America the Beautiful cover nothing here. It's the single most common wrong-pass mistake at this gate, usually made by people who hiked in the Cleveland or San Bernardino forests the week before.

sections.heading: The free-parking reality on N Torrey Pines Rd
sections.body: It's real, and it's oversold. There is free public street parking along N Torrey Pines Rd outside the entrance, facing the ocean, and along Carmel Valley Rd — and because there is no pedestrian entry fee, you can park there and simply walk in. The reserve even points out that visitors who don't want to pay the day-use fee can be dropped off near the bus stop by the free parking area. The catch is supply and distance. Those spaces are shared with Torrey Pines State Beach traffic, surfers and the golf-course crowd, and on a warm weekend they're gone by mid-morning, at which point "free parking on N Torrey Pines Rd" means a space several hundred yards farther south and a walk back along the highway. Then you still have the climb up the park road if you want the mesa trails. Budget it as a real hike, not a shortcut. What it's excellent for: an early weekday morning, a sunset walk on the beach trail, or anyone visiting often enough that $20 a pop stings but $195 a year doesn't pencil out either.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: In priority order. Go early — through the gate before 8am on a weekend and none of the above applies to you. Take the street and walk in — free, always available in the sense that pedestrians are never turned away, and the beach trail from the bottom is arguably the better approach anyway. Use the North Beach lot off Carmel Valley Rd, which draws a different crowd and prices separately, and walk south along the sand at low tide. And the timing move nobody uses: come late. The 10am–1pm peak clears out through the afternoon, gates don't close until sunset, and the light on the sandstone in the last hour is the reason to be here in the first place. Check the tide before you count on the beach route, bring water — there's no shade to speak of on the mesa — and leave the dog at home, because dogs are prohibited throughout the reserve, including the beach below it.

faq.q: How much is parking at Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve?
faq.a: It's demand-based and changes with the day and season. California State Parks lists the North Beach lot at $10–$25 per vehicle and the South Beach lot from $12 up to the same ceiling, with the highest rates on peak weekends and holidays. An annual California State Parks Vehicle Day Use Pass costs $195 at the entrance kiosk.

faq.q: Is there free parking at Torrey Pines?
faq.a: Yes — free street parking on N Torrey Pines Rd outside the entrance and along Carmel Valley Rd, and there is no fee to enter the reserve on foot. The spaces are shared with beach and golf traffic and typically fill by mid-morning on warm weekends, so it works best early or late in the day.

faq.q: Why is the road to the Torrey Pines upper parking lot closed?
faq.a: The two small lots on the mesa near the Lodge fill quickly, and when they do, staff gate the park road at the bottom and signal it with a light above the entrance kiosk. Once that happens you park at the South Beach lot below and walk up. There is also a scheduled closure of the park road and upper lot on the 3rd Wednesday of every month, 7am–1pm.

faq.q: What time does Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve open?
faq.a: Gates open at 7:15am and close at sunset. The visitor center in the Lodge is open 9am to 5pm. The reserve's busiest hours are roughly 10am to 1pm.

faq.q: Does an Adventure Pass work at Torrey Pines?
faq.a: No. Torrey Pines is a California State Natural Reserve, not a national forest, so the Adventure Pass and America the Beautiful passes are not accepted. You need to pay the demand-based day-use fee or hold a California State Parks annual vehicle day use pass.

properties.name: Zion Canyon Visitor Center
properties.meta_title: Zion National Park Parking: The 350-Space Lot, Springdale & the Shuttle (2026)
properties.meta_description: Where to actually park at Zion. The Visitor Center lot has 350+ free spaces and fills by early morning February–November. Springdale's paid zones run $15–$25/day with a free town shuttle. Adventure Pass is not valid here.
properties.card_summary: 350+ free spaces at the South Entrance — full by early-to-mid morning most of the year. Springdale's paid lots ($15–$25) plus a free town shuttle are the real plan B.
properties.quick_answer: The Visitor Center lot is the only large free parking in the park, and it fills early. NPS publishes over 350 spaces (a dozen-plus accessible) at the South Entrance, and says plainly that from February through November parking often fills by early or mid-morning. Entrance is $35 per vehicle for 7 days; America the Beautiful covers it. When it's full, the answer is Springdale: metered on-street zones at $25 (Zone A) / $20 (Zone B) / $15 (Zone C) per day, plus a free town shuttle to the park's pedestrian entrance. Note for SoCal hikers: an Adventure Pass is not valid at Zion — it's a national-forest pass, and this is a national park.
properties.trail_served: Shuttle hub for all of Zion Canyon — Angels Landing (via The Grotto), The Narrows (via Temple of Sinawava), Emerald Pools, Watchman Trail from the lot itself
properties.fee: Parking free; park entrance $35/vehicle (7 days), $30 motorcycle, $20/person. America the Beautiful ($80) accepted. Zion annual $70. Non-US residents 16+ pay a $100 surcharge unless holding an annual or America the Beautiful pass
properties.lot_size: Over 350 spaces, more than a dozen accessible (NPS figure). Human History Museum adds 51; Nature Center is down to 16 during construction
properties.fill_time: NPS: "parking often fills by early or mid-morning" February–November. Visitor reports put the free lots gone between 7 and 9am on busy days
properties.overflow: Springdale paid parking ($15–$25/day by zone) + free Springdale town shuttle to the pedestrian entrance; SunTran bus from St. George/Hurricane
properties.tow_risk: NPS states illegally parked vehicles are subject to citation and/or towing. Do not park on vegetation, roadsides, or wait idling for a space
properties.road_access: Paved — SR-9 through Springdale to the South Entrance
properties.best_season: Shuttle season March 7 – Nov 28, 2026. Private vehicles get the Scenic Drive back Nov 29 – Dec 25 and after Jan 2
properties.address: Zion Canyon Visitor Center, 1 Zion Park Blvd, Springdale, UT 84767

sections.heading: The one thing to understand about Zion
sections.body: You cannot drive the Zion Canyon Scenic Drive during shuttle season. That's the whole system. From March 7 through November 28, 2026, the road to The Grotto, Zion Lodge, and Temple of Sinawava is closed to private vehicles — you park once, at the Visitor Center or in Springdale, and ride the free shuttle from there. So "parking at Zion" really means parking at the South Entrance. NPS lists over 350 spaces at the Zion Canyon Visitor Center, 51 more at the Human History Museum, and 16 at the Nature Center during construction. That's it for free parking inside the park, and NPS is blunt about the math: February through November, it fills by early or mid-morning. In December, January, and February the park sometimes closes the Scenic Drive outright once no legal parking remains.

sections.heading: Plan B: Springdale
sections.body: Springdale is engineered for exactly this. Paid on-street parking runs 6am–5pm daily in three zones — Zone A $25/day (closest to the entrance, through town shuttle stop 4), Zone B $20 (Sundance Rd to Anasazi Way), Zone C $15 (Lion Blvd) — with half-day rates after 1pm and meters that accept up to three consecutive days. The Bit and Spur off-street lot at 1212 Zion Park Blvd is $20 all day, 6am–4pm. Oversized vehicles are Zone C only, $30. After 5pm on-street parking is free. The Springdale town shuttle is free, runs nine stops along Zion Park Boulevard every 10–15 minutes, and drops you at the park's pedestrian entrance where you walk in and catch the canyon shuttle. A round trip on the town line is 20–30 minutes. Private hotel and business lots in town also sell day parking, typically in the same $20–$30 range. Honest version: if you're not in the Visitor Center lot by about 8am in season, budget $20 and skip the circling.

sections.heading: The pass question (read this if you came from a SoCal forest page)
sections.body: Zion charges a national park entrance fee: $35 per private vehicle good for 7 days, $30 motorcycle, $20 per person on foot or bike. An America the Beautiful interagency pass ($80) covers it, as do the Senior, Access, Military, and 4th Grade interagency passes. A Zion-only annual pass is $70. What does not work: the Adventure Pass. It's a Southern California national forest day-use pass and has no standing at a National Park Service entrance station. If you've been hiking on an Adventure Pass all spring and are driving to Zion, you're paying at the gate or upgrading to America the Beautiful. There is currently no timed-entry or reservation system to enter Zion — the only reservation in the park is the Angels Landing permit.

faq.q: Where do you park at Zion National Park?
faq.a: At the Zion Canyon Visitor Center just inside the South Entrance — over 350 free spaces — and then ride the free shuttle into the canyon. If it's full, park in Springdale ($15–$25/day depending on zone) and take the free town shuttle to the pedestrian entrance.

faq.q: What time does Zion's parking lot fill up?
faq.a: NPS says parking often fills by early or mid-morning from February through November. Visitor reports on busy summer and fall days put the free lots gone somewhere between 7 and 9am. Arrive at opening or plan on Springdale.

faq.q: Can you drive your own car in Zion Canyon?
faq.a: Not during shuttle season. In 2026 the shuttle runs March 7 through November 28, and the Zion Canyon Scenic Drive is closed to private vehicles that whole time. Private vehicles are allowed on the Scenic Drive from November 29 to December 25 and after January 2, until the shuttle restarts in spring.

faq.q: How much does it cost to enter Zion National Park?
faq.a: $35 per private vehicle for 7 days, $30 for a motorcycle, or $20 per person on foot or bicycle. An America the Beautiful pass ($80) covers entry. Non-US residents aged 16 and older pay an additional $100 surcharge unless they hold an annual or America the Beautiful pass.

faq.q: Is an Adventure Pass valid at Zion?
faq.a: No. The Adventure Pass is a Southern California national-forest parking pass and is not accepted at any national park entrance, including Zion. You need the $35 vehicle entrance fee or an America the Beautiful interagency pass.

related.label: Temple of Sinawava — The Narrows access

properties.name: Angels Landing / The Grotto (Shuttle Stop 6)
properties.meta_title: Angels Landing Parking & Permits: You Can't Drive There (2026)
properties.meta_description: There is no Angels Landing parking lot. The Grotto trailhead is shuttle-only March 7–Nov 28, 2026. The permit lottery costs $6 to apply plus $3 per person — and it does nothing for your parking. How both systems actually work.
properties.card_summary: No lot, no drive-up. Shuttle-only to The Grotto in season, and a $6 + $3/person permit lottery that buys you exactly zero parking privileges.
properties.quick_answer: There is no Angels Landing parking lot, and your permit doesn't get you one. The trail starts at The Grotto, shuttle stop 6, on a road closed to private vehicles from March 7 to November 28, 2026. You park at the Visitor Center or in Springdale like everyone else, then ride in. The permit is separate from everything: $6 to apply (covers up to 6 people) plus $3 per person if you win. It is not a parking pass, not an entrance fee, and not a shuttle ticket. Rangers check it at The Grotto, at Scout Lookout, and on the chains.
properties.trail_served: Angels Landing via West Rim Trail (5.4 mi RT, chains section permit-required); Scout Lookout and the West Rim Trail are permit-free from the same stop
properties.fee: Park entrance $35/vehicle. Permit: $6 non-refundable application (up to 6 people) + $3 per person if awarded. Shuttle is free
properties.lot_size: None — The Grotto has no public parking during shuttle season. It's a shuttle stop with a picnic area and restrooms
properties.fill_time: N/A for parking. The binding constraint is the permit lottery, then the morning shuttle queue at the Visitor Center — expect a line before 8am in summer
properties.overflow: Park at the Visitor Center (350+ spaces) or Springdale ($15–$25/day) — see the Zion parking master page
properties.tow_risk: You physically cannot drive the Scenic Drive in season; in winter, park only in the marked Grotto pullout spaces
properties.road_access: Zion Canyon Scenic Drive — shuttle only Mar 7–Nov 28, 2026; drivable Nov 29–Dec 25 and after Jan 2
properties.best_season: Spring and fall. Winter is the only time you can drive to the trailhead, but ice on the chains is a real hazard

sections.heading: The permit is not a parking pass
sections.body: People conflate three separate transactions at Zion, and Angels Landing is where it hurts. Entrance fee ($35/vehicle, or America the Beautiful) gets your car through the gate. Parking is first-come at the Visitor Center or paid in Springdale. The Angels Landing permit is a lottery-issued authorization to walk the chains section past Scout Lookout — and it grants you nothing else. Winning it does not reserve a space, does not skip the shuttle line, and does not cover entry. Rangers may check the permit anywhere — NPS specifically names The Grotto, Scout Lookout, and the trail itself. Carry it. Without it, they may turn you around.

sections.heading: How the lottery actually works in 2026
sections.body: Two lotteries, both on Recreation.gov. Seasonal lottery — apply months ahead, rank up to seven date/time options. 2026 windows: Feb 13–25 for March–May hikes; Apr 1–20 for June–August; Jul 1–20 for September 1–November 30 (permits issued July 25); Oct 1–20 for December 1, 2026 – February 28, 2027. $6 to apply, covering up to six people, non-refundable. If you win, $3 per person — refundable if you cancel at least two days out. Day-before lottery — opens 12:01am and closes 3pm Mountain Time, results at 4pm the same day, for the following day's hike. Same $6 application fee, and here everything is non-refundable with no cancellation. This is the real option for anyone already in town, and it's how a lot of people get on the rock.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: Scout Lookout is free and permit-free. The West Rim Trail up to Scout Lookout — Walter's Wiggles, the switchbacks, the view down the canyon — requires no permit at all. It's the same trailhead, the same shuttle, and about 4 miles round trip. Only the final half-mile of chains is gated by the lottery. If your application loses, you have not lost your day. The other honest Plan B is timing: in winter (Nov 29–Dec 25 and after Jan 2 in the 2026–27 season) the Scenic Drive reopens to private vehicles and you can simply drive to The Grotto and park in the small pullout. Permits are still required year-round, and the December–February lottery window opens October 1. The catch is ice — the chains section in winter is a genuinely different hike, and the reason the drive-up is available is the same reason most people shouldn't take it.

faq.q: Where do you park for Angels Landing?
faq.a: You don't park at the trailhead. From March 7 to November 28, 2026, the Zion Canyon Scenic Drive is closed to private vehicles, so you park at the Zion Canyon Visitor Center (350+ free spaces) or in paid Springdale parking ($15–$25/day) and ride the free shuttle to The Grotto, stop 6.

faq.q: Does an Angels Landing permit include parking or park entry?
faq.a: No. The permit only authorizes the chains section past Scout Lookout. You still pay the $35 vehicle entrance fee (or use an America the Beautiful pass) and still find parking on your own. The permit gives you no priority in the lot or the shuttle line.

faq.q: How much does the Angels Landing permit cost?
faq.a: $6 to apply to either lottery, covering an application for up to six people, non-refundable. If you're awarded a permit, it's an additional $3 per person. The seasonal lottery's per-person fee is refundable if you cancel at least two days before your hike; the day-before lottery's fees are not refundable at all.

faq.q: Can you hike to Scout Lookout without a permit?
faq.a: Yes. Only the chains section beyond Scout Lookout requires a permit. The West Rim Trail up to Scout Lookout — roughly 4 miles round trip from The Grotto, including Walter's Wiggles — is open to anyone, no lottery needed.

faq.q: When is the day-before Angels Landing lottery?
faq.a: It opens at 12:01am and closes at 3pm Mountain Time each day, with results issued at 4pm the same day for the next day's hike. It costs $6 to apply and covers up to six people.

related.label: Zion National Park parking — where to leave the car
Temple of Sinawava — the other shuttle-only trailhead

properties.name: Temple of Sinawava (Shuttle Stop 9)
properties.meta_title: The Narrows Parking: Temple of Sinawava Is Shuttle-Only (2026)
properties.meta_description: There's no parking at Temple of Sinawava during shuttle season — it's stop 9 on a road closed to cars March 7–Nov 28, 2026. Where to actually leave the car, plus the 150 CFS closure rule and Zion's active cyanobacteria advisory.
properties.card_summary: The Narrows trailhead has no lot in season — it's shuttle stop 9, a 45-minute ride from the Visitor Center. Closes above 150 CFS, and a cyanobacteria advisory is active park-wide.
properties.quick_answer: Cyanobacteria advisory is active park-wide: NPS says do not submerge your head in or drink water from any river in Zion. The Narrows also closes entirely when the Virgin River exceeds 150 CFS or under a flash flood warning — check the USGS gauge the morning you go. On parking: there isn't any here. Temple of Sinawava is shuttle stop 9, the last one, on a road closed to private vehicles March 7 – November 28, 2026. You park at the Visitor Center or Springdale and ride ~45 minutes each way.
properties.trail_served: Riverside Walk (1 mi paved, mostly accessible) and The Narrows bottom-up from the Temple of Sinawava
properties.fee: Park entrance $35/vehicle (America the Beautiful accepted). Shuttle free. No permit for bottom-up Narrows day hiking
properties.lot_size: No public parking in shuttle season — it's a bus stop with restrooms. In winter, a modest paved turnaround lot that fills fast
properties.fill_time: N/A in season. The real clock is the shuttle: the last-stop ride is ~45 minutes each way, and morning queues at the Visitor Center start before 8am in summer
properties.overflow: Visitor Center lot (350+ spaces) or Springdale paid zones ($15–$25/day) — this is the parking decision, made 8 miles earlier
properties.tow_risk: Not applicable in season — the road is closed to you. In winter, use marked spaces only; NPS cites and tows illegally parked vehicles
properties.road_access: End of Zion Canyon Scenic Drive; shuttle only Mar 7–Nov 28, 2026, drivable Nov 29–Dec 25 and after Jan 2
properties.best_season: Late spring through early fall for wading. Spring runoff regularly pushes flows past the 150 CFS closure line

sections.heading: The parking question has already been answered
sections.body: Temple of Sinawava is stop 9 — the end of the line — on the Zion Canyon Scenic Drive, and during shuttle season that road belongs to the buses. From March 7 through November 28, 2026 there is no version of this where you drive up and park at the Riverside Walk trailhead. So the decision that matters happens at the South Entrance: Visitor Center lot (350+ free spaces, gone by early-to-mid morning per NPS) or Springdale's paid zones at $15–$25/day with a free town shuttle in. From the Visitor Center, the shuttle takes about 45 minutes to reach Temple of Sinawava, with buses every 5–10 minutes. Build 90 minutes of round-trip bus time into your day and don't be the person sprinting for the last departure — in the peak schedule the final bus leaves the Temple at 8:15pm.

sections.heading: The two things that close The Narrows
sections.body: Flow. NPS closes The Narrows to all travel when the Virgin River runs over 150 cubic feet per second, and during flash flood warnings. Below 70 CFS is the comfortable range with knee-deep crossings; above 70 it's mid-thigh and genuinely hard work. The USGS gauge at Virgin River near Springdale updates continuously — check it the morning of, not the week before. Spring runoff blows past 150 routinely. Cyanobacteria. Park biologists have found toxic cyanobacteria in the Virgin River, and the advisory is current: don't drink the water, don't filter it and drink it, and don't submerge your head. This does not close the hike — people wade The Narrows daily under it — but it does mean packing every drop of drinking water you'll need, which is heavier than most first-timers plan for.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: If the river's too high or the day's too short, the Riverside Walk is the answer that's already at the same stop: a paved, mostly accessible one-mile path along the Virgin River to where The Narrows begins. It's the best scenery-per-effort in Zion and it stays open when the canyon itself doesn't. If you want to actually drive to this trailhead, that window is November 29 – December 25 and after January 2, when the Scenic Drive reopens to private vehicles. The parking at the Temple is a modest turnaround lot that fills quickly, and winter Narrows hiking requires a drysuit rental from Springdale — but you will have the place to yourself. And when the shuttle isn't running and the canyon has no legal parking left, NPS has been known to close the Scenic Drive entirely, so start early even in December.

faq.q: Where do you park for The Narrows in Zion?
faq.a: During shuttle season (March 7–November 28, 2026) you can't park at the trailhead. Park at the Zion Canyon Visitor Center or in Springdale and ride the free shuttle to Temple of Sinawava, stop 9 — about a 45-minute ride from the Visitor Center.

faq.q: When does The Narrows close?
faq.a: NPS closes The Narrows to all travel when the Virgin River flow exceeds 150 cubic feet per second, and during flash flood warnings. Check the USGS gauge for the Virgin River near Springdale the morning you plan to hike.

faq.q: Is there a cyanobacteria warning at Zion right now?
faq.a: Yes. NPS advises visitors not to drink water from or submerge their heads in any river in Zion because of toxic cyanobacteria. The advisory does not close The Narrows, but you must carry all your drinking water — filtering river water is not safe against these toxins.

faq.q: Do you need a permit for The Narrows?
faq.a: Not for the bottom-up day hike from Temple of Sinawava, which is what most visitors do. The top-down through-hike from Chamberlain's Ranch is a separate wilderness permit.

faq.q: How long does the shuttle take to Temple of Sinawava?
faq.a: About 45 minutes each way from the Zion Canyon Visitor Center, making a round trip roughly an hour and a half of bus time before you hike anything. Shuttles run every 5–10 minutes in the canyon.

related.label: Zion National Park parking — where to leave the car

properties.name: Canyon Overlook Trailhead
properties.meta_title: Canyon Overlook Trail Parking: Tiny Lot Just East of the Tunnel (2026)
properties.meta_description: Canyon Overlook is the Zion trailhead you can actually drive to — but the lot east of the Zion–Mt Carmel Tunnel is tiny and gridlocks by mid-morning. Plus the June 2026 rule that bans large vehicles from the highway entirely.
properties.card_summary: The one big Zion hike you drive to — into a lot NPS itself warns you may have to circle for. And as of NPS's June 2026 rules, big rigs can't reach it at all.
properties.quick_answer: The only marquee Zion hike you can still drive to — into a genuinely tiny lot. It sits on Highway 9 immediately east of the Zion–Mt Carmel Tunnel, open year-round with no shuttle. NPS doesn't publish a space count; it just warns you should expect several passes before finding a spot. Pit toilets on site. New for 2026: vehicles over 35'9" long, 7'10" wide, 11'4" tall or 50,000 lbs are banned outright from the highway between Canyon Junction and the East Entrance — NPS's large-vehicle page, updated June 2026, says they "will not be permitted." It no longer offers the old $15 tunnel escort as a way through.
properties.trail_served: Canyon Overlook Trail — 1 mile round trip, 154 ft gain, moderate; sandstone steps up to 18", tread as narrow as 12", overhead clearance as low as 4 ft. No pets
properties.fee: Park entrance $35/vehicle (America the Beautiful accepted). No separate parking fee, no permit
properties.lot_size: Small paved lot on the south side of Hwy 9 — NPS publishes no space count and tells visitors to expect multiple attempts. Visitor reports describe roughly 8–12 spaces
properties.fill_time: Fills quickly through the busy summer months; earlier than you'd like on any pleasant morning. Early morning or late afternoon are the reliable windows
properties.overflow: Several small pullouts further east on Hwy 9 — legal, but you walk back along a road with no shoulder and steady tunnel traffic
properties.tow_risk: Park fully in designated spaces or established pullouts; NPS cites and tows vehicles parked on roadsides and vegetation
properties.road_access: Paved Hwy 9 (Zion–Mt Carmel Highway), immediately east of the tunnel's east portal. Open year-round; watch for ice on the ledges in winter
properties.best_season: Year-round. Sunset is the classic time and also the second crush of the day

sections.heading: A real trailhead lot, and it's the whole problem
sections.body: Canyon Overlook is the exception in Zion: it's on the Zion–Mt Carmel Highway, not the Scenic Drive, so no shuttle serves it and none is needed. You drive up, park, and walk half a mile to a railed sandstone ledge looking straight down the switchbacks into Zion Canyon. One mile round trip, 154 feet of gain, and one of the best payoff-to-effort ratios in the park — which is exactly why the parking is what it is. The lot is a small paved strip on the south side of Highway 9 right where the tunnel spits you out eastbound. NPS does not publish a capacity figure, and we won't invent one — what the agency does say is that visitors should anticipate multiple attempts before securing a spot. Hiker accounts consistently describe something in the neighborhood of a dozen spaces. Pit toilets are at the lot. The trailhead itself is across the road from the parking, so watch for traffic exiting the tunnel.

sections.heading: The June 2026 rule that changed the drive
sections.body: This matters if you're in anything larger than a car. As of NPS's June 2026 update, vehicles exceeding 35 feet 9 inches in length, 7 feet 10 inches in width, 11 feet 4 inches in height, or 50,000 pounds are no longer permitted on the Zion–Mt Carmel Highway between Canyon Junction and the East Entrance. Combined vehicles are capped at 50 feet overall with a 26-foot trailer. Measurements include mirrors, tires, and attachments. This appears to replace the tunnel escort system. NPS no longer lists the escort service, or the $15 tunnel permit that used to hold traffic and walk oversize rigs through one at a time; the current page simply says over-limit vehicles will not be permitted, and offers no exception or workaround. NPS doesn't spell out the permit's retirement in so many words, so if your trip depends on it, confirm at (435) 772-3256. NPS cited a 2016 study finding the escort system left an average of only 19 minutes of free-flowing traffic per hour at the tunnel, and 8 minutes on the worst day measured. If your rig is over the limit, you cannot reach Canyon Overlook from the west, and you cannot use SR-9 as a through route; the detours are Highway 20 to Bryce (~150 miles) or Highway 59 toward the North Rim (~128 miles).

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: Go east. If the lot is full, continue on Highway 9 — there are several small established pullouts further east, and people park there and walk back. Be honest about that walk: Highway 9 here is narrow, shoulderless, and full of drivers who just came out of a dark tunnel. It's fine at 7am and unpleasant at 2pm. Or go later. The two clean windows are early morning and the hour or so before the sunset crowd arrives. Mid-morning through mid-afternoon is when the tunnel backs up and the lot gridlocks. Or go elsewhere on the east side. The Zion–Mt Carmel Highway east of the tunnel is full of slickrock pullouts with no crowds at all, and the entrance fee you already paid covers all of it. If you're set on Canyon Overlook and it's noon in July, the realistic move is to come back at 6am tomorrow.

faq.q: Where do you park for the Canyon Overlook Trail?
faq.a: In the small lot on the south side of Highway 9 immediately east of the Zion–Mt Carmel Tunnel. The trailhead is across the road from the lot. Pit toilets are available in the parking area.

faq.q: How many parking spaces are at Canyon Overlook?
faq.a: NPS does not publish a number — it simply warns visitors to expect several attempts before finding a spot. Visitor reports describe roughly a dozen spaces. It is one of the smallest developed trailhead lots in the park.

faq.q: Do you need the shuttle for Canyon Overlook?
faq.a: No. Canyon Overlook is on the Zion–Mt Carmel Highway, not the Zion Canyon Scenic Drive, so it's drivable year-round and no shuttle serves it. This makes it the go-to hike when Zion Canyon's shuttle logistics don't fit your day.

faq.q: Can you drive an RV to Canyon Overlook in 2026?
faq.a: Only if it's under the limits. Per NPS's June 2026 large-vehicle page, vehicles longer than 35 feet 9 inches, wider than 7 feet 10 inches, taller than 11 feet 4 inches, or over 50,000 pounds are banned from the Zion–Mt Carmel Highway between Canyon Junction and the East Entrance. NPS no longer offers the $15 tunnel escort permit as a way through; confirm at (435) 772-3256 if your rig is near the limits.

faq.q: What if the Canyon Overlook lot is full?
faq.a: Continue east on Highway 9 to one of several small pullouts and walk back — but the road has essentially no shoulder and heavy tunnel traffic, so it's only reasonable early or late. Otherwise, come back at dawn.

Kolob Canyons — the other drive-yourself entrance
Large vehicle rules on the Zion–Mt Carmel Highway (NPS)

properties.name: Kolob Canyons Visitor Center
properties.meta_title: Kolob Canyons Parking (Zion): Drive Yourself, No Shuttle, No Crowd (2026)
properties.meta_description: Kolob Canyons is Zion's separate entrance at I-15 Exit 40 — own visitor center, own lot, no shuttle, drive the whole 5-mile scenic road yourself. Same $35 entrance fee, a fraction of the people.
properties.card_summary: Zion without the shuttle: separate entrance at I-15 Exit 40, park at trailheads directly, drive the 5-mile scenic road. Road reopened in 2023 after a collapse and has been open since.
properties.quick_answer: The Zion where you still just park at the trailhead. Kolob Canyons has its own entrance at I-15 Exit 40 — 40 miles north of Zion Canyon, 17 south of Cedar City — its own visitor center and lot, and no shuttle system at all. You drive the five-mile scenic road and park at viewpoints and trailheads directly. Same $35 vehicle entrance fee (America the Beautiful accepted), collected at the Kolob Canyons Visitor Center — everyone must stop. Visitor center hours are 8am–12pm and 1pm–5pm, closed over the lunch hour. The road closed for a collapse in 2023 and reopened November 29, 2023; it's been open since, but still shuts for winter snow and ice.
properties.trail_served: Over 20 miles of trails — Taylor Creek Middle Fork, Timber Creek Overlook, Lee Pass to Kolob Arch. Groups over 12 are restricted to Timber Creek Overlook
properties.fee: Park entrance $35/vehicle for 7 days (America the Beautiful accepted). All visitors must stop at the Kolob Canyons Visitor Center to pay or show a pass
properties.lot_size: Visitor center lot plus individual trailhead and viewpoint lots along the 5-mile road. No published space counts — but demand here is a fraction of Zion Canyon's
properties.fill_time: Rarely the story. Trailhead lots at Taylor Creek and Lee Pass can fill on peak spring weekends; Zion Canyon this is not
properties.overflow: Generally unnecessary. If a trailhead lot is full, the next viewpoint down the road usually isn't
properties.tow_risk: Low — use designated spaces; standard NPS citation/tow policy applies
properties.road_access: Paved Kolob Canyons Road, 5 miles from I-15 Exit 40. Reopened Nov 29, 2023 after rehabilitation; may close in winter for snow or ice
properties.best_season: Spring for Taylor Creek's wildflowers and flowing water, fall for color. Check road status before winter trips
properties.address: Kolob Canyons Visitor Center, 3752 E Kolob Canyon Rd, New Harmony, UT 84757 (I-15 Exit 40)

sections.heading: A different park, on the same ticket
sections.body: Kolob Canyons is administratively Zion and functionally somewhere else. It has a separate entrance off I-15 at Exit 40, no connection by road to Zion Canyon (it's about a 40-mile drive between them), its own visitor center, and — the point — no shuttle. The whole apparatus that governs a Zion Canyon day, the 350-space lot, the bus queue, the 8am scramble, simply doesn't exist here. You drive the five-mile Kolob Canyons Road yourself and park where you're hiking. Every visitor must stop at the Kolob Canyons Visitor Center to pay the entrance fee or display an interagency pass. It's open 8am–12pm and 1pm–5pm — note the lunch closure, which catches people. One limitation worth knowing: wilderness permits are only issued at the Zion Canyon Visitor Center, 40 miles away, so if your plan needs one, sort it out first.

sections.heading: Road status, honestly
sections.body: Kolob Canyons Road has a recent history worth knowing about. In March 2023 rangers spotted a depression in the pavement and closed the road as a precaution; it then began to collapse. NPS and Federal Highway Administration engineers rebuilt it — excavating and reinforcing the base eight feet deep with geo-grid and compacted soil, repaving over 6,000 square feet, and rebuilding drainage — and the road reopened November 29, 2023 with full access past South Fork Picnic Area to the Lee Pass Trailhead, Kolob Canyons Picnic Area, and Kolob Viewpoint. It has been open since. The remaining caveat is seasonal: NPS says the road may close during winter months due to snow or ice, and that pedestrians can't walk it during plowing. Check the park's conditions page before a December-to-March trip. (Different road, common confusion: Kolob Terrace Road — the one to Lava Point and the Subway — typically closes fall through spring at Maloney Hill.)

sections.heading: Plan B — this is the Plan B
sections.body:
Kolob Canyons is what you do when Zion Canyon has already beaten you. If you rolled up to the South Entrance at 10am in October and the Visitor Center lot was full, Springdale wanted $25, and the shuttle line was 200 people deep, Kolob is 40 miles north on the same 7-day entrance receipt and you will park at the trailhead.

What's there: over 20 miles of trail, the Timber Creek Overlook (a flat mile round trip from the road's end), Taylor Creek Middle Fork to the Double Arch Alcove, and the long haul from Lee Pass toward Kolob Arch. The Finger Canyons in late-day light are the reason photographers make the drive on purpose rather than as a fallback. If you're arriving from I-15 anyway — coming down from Salt Lake or up from Vegas — it's not a consolation prize, it's a better first day.

faq.q: Do you need a shuttle for Kolob Canyons?
faq.a: No. Kolob Canyons has no shuttle system. You drive the entire five-mile Kolob Canyons Road yourself and park at the viewpoints and trailheads, year-round when the road is open.

faq.q: Is Kolob Canyons Road open?
faq.a: Yes — it reopened November 29, 2023 after a rehabilitation project that followed a 2023 road collapse, and has been open since. NPS notes it may still close during winter months due to snow or ice, so check current conditions before a cold-season trip.

faq.q: Does your Zion entrance fee cover Kolob Canyons?
faq.a: Yes. It's the same park. The $35 per-vehicle fee is valid for 7 days at all Zion entrances, and America the Beautiful passes are accepted. You must stop at the Kolob Canyons Visitor Center to pay or show your pass.

faq.q: How far is Kolob Canyons from Zion Canyon?
faq.a: About 40 miles by road — Kolob Canyons is at Interstate 15 Exit 40, roughly 17 miles south of Cedar City. There is no road connection through the park between the two areas.

faq.q: Can you get wilderness permits at Kolob Canyons?
faq.a: No. Wilderness permits are issued only at the Zion Canyon Visitor Center near the South Entrance, so arrange any permit you need before driving to Kolob.

properties.name: Bryce Canyon Visitor Center
properties.meta_title: Bryce Canyon Parking (2026): The Shuttle Is Free AND Optional — You Can Drive
properties.meta_description: Where to park at Bryce Canyon. Unlike Zion, the shuttle is optional and every viewpoint stays open to private cars. $35/vehicle for 7 days, no timed entry, no reservations. But the Visitor Center lot is 1-hour parking only.
properties.card_summary: The anti-Zion. Free shuttle you never have to ride, no reservation system, and you can drive to all four amphitheater viewpoints — but the Visitor Center lot is a 1-hour lot, not a hiker lot.
properties.quick_answer: If you came here from our Zion pages, unlearn the shuttle. At Bryce Canyon the shuttle is free and completely optional — NPS states flatly that "in no area of the park is riding the shuttle mandatory" and that no reservations are required for it. Bryce Point, Inspiration Point, Sunset Point, and Sunrise Point all stay open to private vehicles year-round. There is no road closed to your car the way Zion Canyon Scenic Drive is. There is also no timed entry. NPS: "There are no advanced reservations required to enter Bryce Canyon National Park." Pay $35 per vehicle, good 7 days, and drive in. The one trap is the lot you're aiming at: Visitor Center parking is 1-hour only. Hikers are supposed to use the Additional Parking Lot across the road, or the free all-day lot at the Shuttle Station in Bryce Canyon City. And note for SoCal drivers — an Adventure Pass is not valid here; it's a national-forest pass.
properties.trail_served: Hub for the whole Bryce Amphitheater — Navajo Loop and Queen's/Navajo Combination from Sunset Point, Queen's Garden from Sunrise Point, Peekaboo Loop and Hat Shop from Bryce Point, Fairyland Loop from Fairyland Point, plus the 5.5-mile Rim Trail
properties.fee: Parking free everywhere in the park. Entrance $35/vehicle (7 days), $30 motorcycle, $20 per person 16+ (under 16 free). Bryce Canyon annual $70. America the Beautiful ($80) accepted, as are Senior, Access, Military and 4th Grade passes. Non-US residents 16+ pay an additional $100 per person unless holding an Annual or America the Beautiful pass. The park does not accept cash — card only, or buy a digital pass on Recreation.gov
properties.lot_size: NPS publishes exact counts for the viewpoint lots (Sunset 115, Sunrise 113, Bryce Point 49, Inspiration 36, Paria View 21, Fairyland 20, Mossy Cave 15) but does not publish a space count for the Visitor Center lot, the Additional Parking Lot across the road, or the Shuttle Station lot. Visitor Center parking is posted 1-hour
properties.fill_time: NPS does not publish a fill time. What it does publish: the amphitheater is "the most congested area of the park," spaces "can be sparse during our peak seasons," and lots that reach capacity "may be temporarily closed until parking becomes available." Peak months are May through September; visitor reports put the squeeze from mid-morning to mid-afternoon
properties.overflow: Free all-day parking at the Shuttle Station in Bryce Canyon City (shuttle Stop 1), and the Additional Parking Lot across from the Visitor Center. Both connect to the free shuttle
properties.tow_risk: Not a towing hotspot — the park's enforcement lever is the 1-hour limit at the Visitor Center and closing full lots at the entrance. Park in marked spaces only; NPS routes oversize vehicles out of the amphitheater lots rather than ticketing them in
properties.road_access: Paved. Highway 63 south from Highway 12; the Visitor Center sits adjacent to the entrance fee booths at the north end of the park
properties.best_season: Open 24 hours a day, year-round. Shuttle runs April 3 – October 18, 2026. Visitor Center 8am–8pm summer, 8am–6pm spring and fall, 8am–4:30pm winter
properties.address: Bryce Canyon Visitor Center, Highway 63, Bryce Canyon City, UT 84764

sections.heading: Bryce is not Zion — read this before you plan the day
sections.body: Half the people who drive from Zion to Bryce arrive braced for the same system, and it isn't the same system. At Zion, the Scenic Drive is closed to private vehicles for most of the year and the shuttle is the only way in. At Bryce, NPS answers its own FAQ this way: "In no area of the park is riding the shuttle mandatory." The parking page says the same thing from the other direction — "Shuttles are offered as a convenience but are not mandatory; no reservations required." Every viewpoint has a lot open to private cars. So the three things Bryce does not have: no mandatory shuttle, no timed-entry reservation (that's Arches you're thinking of), and no shuttle ticket — the bus is included with your entrance fee, and Interagency Annual, Access, and Lifetime pass holders just show the pass to the driver. What Bryce has instead is a straightforward capacity problem in a three-mile stretch of road. The Bryce Amphitheater — Bryce, Inspiration, Sunset, and Sunrise Points — holds a combined 313 parking spaces across its four main lots, and takes the overwhelming share of a park that draws over two million visitors a year. NPS names the cause without hedging: "The number one cause of parking lot congestion is hikers whose cars sit in popular overlook parking lots for multiple hours while they hike. When these parking lots reach capacity, they may be temporarily closed until parking becomes available." That last clause is the one to plan around. A full lot at Bryce doesn't mean circling — it can mean a ranger waving you past the turn.

sections.heading: Where to actually leave the car
sections.body: Not at the Visitor Center, if you're hiking. NPS is explicit on the shuttle page: "If you're boarding here, be sure to park your car across the street in the Additional Parking Lot. Visitor Center parking is 1 hour." The Visitor Center lot is for people watching the 24-minute film and asking a ranger about trail conditions, not for people spending four hours on the Fairyland Loop. The two long-stay options NPS actually recommends: Shuttle Station, Bryce Canyon City (Stop 1) — free all-day parking, outside the park entirely. You buy your pass and board there with a shorter line. This is the recommended play in July and August. Additional Parking Lot, across from the Visitor Center — inside the fee gate, all-day, and the designated place for oversize rigs. On oversize: vehicles 23 feet (7 m) or longer are barred during shuttle hours from the Visitor Center 1-hour lot, Sunrise Point, Bryce Lodge, Sunset Point, Inspiration Point, and Bryce Point. Paria View bans them at all hours because the lot is too small to turn around in. If you're in a Class C or towing anything, your parking decision is made for you: Shuttle Station, Additional Parking Lot, or your own campsite/hotel. The southern Scenic Drive past Mile 3 has no such restriction — NPS says all vehicles including oversize "are permitted to drive this road and park at all viewpoints."

sections.heading: The fee, the passes, and the pass that doesn't work
sections.body: $35 per private vehicle, valid 7 days. $30 for a motorcycle, $20 per person on foot or bike (under 16 free). A Bryce Canyon annual pass is $70; America the Beautiful at $80 covers it and every other federal site. Non-US residents aged 16 and over pay an additional $100 per person unless they hold an Annual or America the Beautiful pass — same surcharge structure now in force at Zion. Two practical notes people get caught by. First, the park does not accept cash. Card at the booth, or buy a digital pass on Recreation.gov ahead of time and have the PDF on your phone. Second, if you're boarding the shuttle outside the park at Bryce Canyon City, you need proof of admission to show the driver — the pass is what gets you on the bus. What doesn't work here: the Adventure Pass. It's a Southern California national-forest day-use pass and has no standing at a National Park Service entrance station. If you've been hiking Icehouse Canyon and Chantry Flats on one all spring, it buys you nothing at Bryce. Same answer as at the Zion entrance — and if you're doing both parks in one trip, the $80 America the Beautiful pays for itself on the second gate.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: Bryce's Plan B is unusually good, which is the compensation for the small lots. Ride the bus you didn't have to ride. The 2026 shuttle runs April 3 – October 18: 8am–6pm April 3–May 8, 8am–8pm May 9–September 20, 8am–6pm September 21–October 18. Buses come about every 15 minutes, the full 15-stop loop takes roughly 50 minutes, and there's a live shuttle tracker. Park free at the Shuttle Station, ride in, and the parking problem stops being yours. Pets aren't allowed on board — that's the one real disqualifier. Go south instead. The Scenic Drive from Mile 3 to Rainbow Point has no shuttle and much less pressure: Rainbow Point has 56 spaces, Natural Bridge 30, Swamp Canyon 11, Agua Canyon 7. Oversize vehicles are welcome at all of them. Go north to Mossy Cave. Fifteen spaces on Highway 12, a short trail, and no entrance booth to queue at — see the Mossy Cave page. Or just come at the right hour. The park is open 24 hours. The amphitheater lots are a mid-day problem; at dawn the constraint is the sunrise crowd at Bryce Point and Sunset Point, and by late afternoon in shoulder season it clears out. Honest version: if you arrive at 11am on a July Saturday and drive straight for Sunset Point, you are going to have a bad twenty minutes.

faq.q: Is the Bryce Canyon shuttle mandatory like Zion's?
faq.a: No. NPS states that in no area of Bryce Canyon is riding the shuttle mandatory, and no reservations are required for it. Every viewpoint — Bryce, Inspiration, Sunset, and Sunrise Points — has a parking lot open to private vehicles year-round. The shuttle is free with park admission and purely a convenience. This is the opposite of Zion, where the Zion Canyon Scenic Drive is closed to private cars during shuttle season.

faq.q: Does Bryce Canyon require a timed entry or reservation?
faq.a: No. NPS says plainly: "There are no advanced reservations required to enter Bryce Canyon National Park." You present a pass or pay the entrance fee at the booth and drive in. People often confuse Bryce with Arches, which does run a timed-entry system in peak months. Bryce does not, and never has.

faq.q: How much does it cost to enter Bryce Canyon in 2026?
faq.a: $35 per private vehicle for 7 days, $30 for a motorcycle, or $20 per person on foot or bicycle (children under 16 free). A Bryce Canyon annual pass is $70 and an America the Beautiful interagency pass ($80) covers entry. Non-US residents aged 16 and older pay an additional $100 per person unless they hold an Annual or America the Beautiful pass. The park does not accept cash — bring a card or buy a digital pass on Recreation.gov.

faq.q: Where should I park at Bryce Canyon if I'm going hiking?
faq.a: Not at the Visitor Center — that lot is posted 1-hour parking. NPS directs all-day parkers to the Additional Parking Lot across the road from the Visitor Center, or to the free all-day lot at the Shuttle Station in Bryce Canyon City. Both are shuttle stops. Viewpoint lots are open to cars, but they are small and NPS may close them temporarily once they reach capacity.

faq.q: Can I park an RV at the Bryce Canyon viewpoints?
faq.a: Not in the amphitheater during shuttle hours. Vehicles 23 feet or longer are restricted from the Visitor Center 1-hour lot, Sunrise Point, Bryce Lodge, Sunset Point, Inspiration Point, and Bryce Point while the shuttle is operating, and from Paria View at all hours. Oversize vehicles should use the Additional Parking Lot, the Shuttle Station lot, or a campsite. The southern Scenic Drive past Mile 3 has no oversize restrictions.

properties.name: Sunset Point
properties.meta_title: Sunset Point Parking, Bryce Canyon: 115 Spaces for the Park's Busiest Trail (2026)
properties.meta_description: Sunset Point has 115 spaces — the biggest lot in the Bryce Amphitheater and still the tightest squeeze, because Navajo Loop and Queen's/Navajo start here. NPS closes the lot when it fills. Wall Street closes in winter.
properties.card_summary: 115 spaces (7 oversize) serving the busiest trailhead in the park. NPS temporarily closes the lot when it hits capacity — and blames hikers whose cars sit there for hours. Which is you.
properties.quick_answer: 115 spaces, 7 of them oversize — an NPS-published count, and the largest lot in the Bryce Amphitheater. It still fails first, because the Navajo Loop (1.3 mi, 515 ft of climbing) and the Queen's/Navajo Combination (2.9 mi, 600 ft) both start here, and those are the two hikes almost everyone at Bryce does. NPS names the mechanism and it's uncomfortably specific: "The number one cause of parking lot congestion is hikers whose cars sit in popular overlook parking lots for multiple hours while they hike. When these parking lots reach capacity, they may be temporarily closed until parking becomes available." So the failure mode here isn't circling — it's being turned away at the turnoff. The free, optional shuttle (Stop 11) exists exactly for this, and Sunrise Point's 113 spaces are a flat half-mile walk away on the paved Rim Trail.

badges.label: Lot can be closed when full

properties.trail_served: Navajo Loop (1.3 mi RT, 515 ft, 1–2 hrs) past Thor's Hammer and Wall Street; Queen's/Navajo Combination Loop (2.9 mi, 600 ft); Rim Trail — the paved, accessible, leashed-pets-allowed 0.5 mi to Sunrise Point and 0.7 mi to Inspiration Point; Figure-8 and Peekaboo connections
properties.fee: Parking free. Park entrance $35/vehicle for 7 days; America the Beautiful accepted. No cash — card or digital pass only. Shuttle free with admission
properties.lot_size: 115 total spaces, 7 oversize, per NPS. The largest of the four amphitheater lots — Sunrise has 113, Inspiration 36, Bryce Point 49
properties.fill_time: NPS publishes no clock time — only that the amphitheater is "the most congested area of the park" and that spaces "can be sparse during our peak seasons" (May–September). Visitor reports consistently describe mid-morning through mid-afternoon as the wall, with the lot workable again in the last hours of daylight
properties.overflow: Sunrise Point (113 spaces), a flat paved 0.5 mi away on the Rim Trail — you can hike Navajo/Queen's from either end. Otherwise the free shuttle from the Shuttle Station in Bryce Canyon City or the Additional Parking Lot at the Visitor Center
properties.tow_risk: Park in marked spaces only. The practical risk here isn't a tow, it's the lot being closed to inbound traffic once it's at capacity
properties.road_access: Paved spur off the main park road at Mile 2.25. Vehicles 23 ft or longer are barred from this lot during shuttle hours
properties.best_season: Year-round; the lot is plowed as part of the priority first three miles. Wall Street on the Navajo Loop closes for the winter — Two Bridges stays open

sections.heading: Why the biggest lot is the worst lot
sections.body: Sunset Point has more spaces than any other viewpoint in the amphitheater — 115, seven of them oversize — and it's still where the day goes wrong. The reason is dwell time. A viewpoint visitor parks for fifteen minutes. A Navajo Loop hiker parks for two hours; a Queen's/Navajo hiker for three; a Figure-8 hiker for five. Sunset Point is the trailhead for all of them. NPS says this out loud rather than dancing around it: hikers' cars sitting for multiple hours are "the number one cause of parking lot congestion," and full lots "may be temporarily closed until parking becomes available." Read that as written. You can be denied the turn, not merely delayed in it. The trails themselves are short by national-park standards, which is part of the problem — they're achievable for everyone. Navajo Loop is 1.3 miles with 515 feet of climbing, one to two hours, with grades touching 30% on the switchbacks. The Queen's/Navajo Combination is 2.9 miles and 600 feet, and is the single most-recommended hike in the park. Two million-plus annual visitors, one obvious hike, one 115-space lot.

sections.heading: Wall Street, winter, and which way you'll actually go
sections.body: The Navajo Loop has two sides. Wall Street — the slot between hundred-foot fins — is the one on the postcards. It closes in winter, and NPS ties the closure to conditions rather than a fixed date: it shuts in months when precipitation combines with freezing overnight temperatures, because of rockfall. When it's closed, you descend and return via the Two Bridges side, which stays open year-round and passes directly under Thor's Hammer. That matters for parking because a closed Wall Street doesn't reduce demand at Sunset Point at all — it just changes the route. Winter is still the easiest time to get a space here, since the lot sits inside the first three miles of road that NPS plows as a priority. After a storm the main road may close to all traffic beyond Mile 3 while crews work the higher elevations; Sunset Point at Mile 2.25 is inside that protected stretch. One more constraint that catches people: if your vehicle is 23 feet or longer, you cannot park here during shuttle hours at all. Same at Sunrise, Inspiration, Bryce Point, and Bryce Lodge.

sections.heading: Plan B
sections.body: Sunrise Point, then walk. This is the good Plan B and almost nobody uses it. Sunrise Point has 113 spaces — two fewer than Sunset — and sits a flat, paved half-mile away on the Rim Trail, the most accessible trail in the park and the one section where leashed pets are allowed. Better still, hike the Queen's/Navajo loop from Sunrise Point instead: same loop, opposite direction, different lot. The free shuttle. Sunset Point is Stop 11. In 2026 the shuttle runs April 3 – October 18, roughly every 15 minutes, 8am–8pm from May 9 to September 20 and 8am–6pm at the shoulders. Park free and all day at the Shuttle Station in Bryce Canyon City and skip the whole question. It costs nothing beyond your entrance fee, and it's the only option if you're driving something over 23 feet. Or change the hour. The park is open 24 hours. Sunset Point at first light or in the last hour before dark is a different place, and the lot behaves accordingly. Honest version: on a summer weekend, arriving here between about 10am and 3pm without a backup plan is the mistake.

faq.q: How many parking spaces are at Sunset Point in Bryce Canyon?
faq.a: 115 total spaces, including 7 for oversize vehicles, according to the National Park Service. That makes it the largest of the four Bryce Amphitheater viewpoint lots — Sunrise Point has 113, Bryce Point 49, and Inspiration Point 36.

faq.q: What time does the Sunset Point parking lot fill up?
faq.a: NPS does not publish a fill time. It says only that the Bryce Amphitheater is the most congested area of the park, that spaces can be sparse in peak season (May through September), and that lots may be temporarily closed once they reach capacity. Visitor reports put the hard window from roughly mid-morning to mid-afternoon on summer days.

faq.q: Can you drive to Sunset Point, or do you have to take the shuttle?
faq.a: You can drive. The Bryce Canyon shuttle is free and optional — NPS states that riding it is not mandatory anywhere in the park. Sunset Point is shuttle Stop 11 but the lot is open to private vehicles year-round. The one exception: vehicles 23 feet or longer cannot park here during shuttle hours.

faq.q: Where does the Navajo Loop trail start?
faq.a: At Sunset Point. The Navajo Loop is 1.3 miles round trip with 515 feet of elevation change, typically one to two hours, and passes Thor's Hammer and the Wall Street slot. Combined with Queen's Garden it becomes the 2.9-mile, 600-foot Queen's/Navajo Combination Loop, which can be started from either Sunset or Sunrise Point.

faq.q: Is Wall Street open in winter?
faq.a: No. The Wall Street side of the Navajo Loop closes during months when precipitation combines with freezing overnight temperatures, due to rockfall risk. The Two Bridges side stays open year-round and can be hiked as an out-and-back or combined with Queen's Garden.
